class_schedule.topic: Globalization: Drivers, Discontents, and the Shifting World
learning_objectives: Define globalization and identify its key drivers: trade liberalization, technological change, capital mobility, and political shifts
Analyze evidence for and against the globalization thesis from the past 40 years
Distinguish globalization of markets from globalization of production
Evaluate contemporary challenges to globalization including populism, pandemics, and geopolitical fragmentation
readings: Hill, International Business: Competing in the Global Marketplace (13th ed.), Ch. 1 - Globalization
Friedman, The World is Flat (updated edition), Ch. 1-3 - The Flattening of the World
Rodrik, The Globalization Paradox (2011), Ch. 1-2 - The Political Trilemma
Baldwin, The Great Convergence (2016), Ch. 1 - The Second Unbundling

lecture_notes.introduction: Globalization is the defining economic story of the past seventy-five years, and it is a story whose ending is still being written. From 1945 to 2010, the world experienced an unprecedented integration of markets, production, capital flows, and information. Global trade grew from 8% of world GDP in 1945 to 25% in 2008. Multinational corporations built production networks spanning dozens of countries. Containers, jumbo jets, satellite communications, and the internet collapsed distances that had constrained commerce for centuries. Billions of people in China, India, Vietnam, Indonesia, and elsewhere were pulled out of extreme poverty by manufacturing work in globally integrated supply chains. Apple designs iPhones in California, has them assembled in China from components made in Taiwan, South Korea, Germany, Japan, and the United States, and sells them in 100+ countries. This is unprecedented in human history. But the story has turned ambiguous since roughly 2010. Trade as a share of GDP peaked in 2008 and has not recovered. The 2008 financial crisis eroded faith in unregulated capital flows. The 2016 Brexit vote and Trump election revealed massive political backlash against globalization in its heartlands. The US-China trade war starting in 2018 began unwinding the most important integration of the past three decades. COVID-19 showed the fragility of just-in-time global supply chains. Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022 showed how geopolitics can shatter economic relationships overnight. The semiconductor export controls of 2022-2026 have effectively bifurcated the world's most important technology industry. Dani Rodrik's 'political trilemma' argument - that deep globalization, national sovereignty, and democratic politics cannot all coexist - looks increasingly prescient. This course begins by understanding what globalization is, what has driven it, what it has achieved, and why it is now in retreat. The frameworks you learn in this course will apply whether the world continues to integrate, fragments into regional blocs, or takes some new path we have not yet seen.
key_concepts: Globalization of markets: the merging of historically distinct national markets into one global marketplace as consumer preferences converge and barriers to cross-border commerce decline
Globalization of production: sourcing goods and services from locations around the world to take advantage of national differences in costs and quality of factors of production

Drivers of globalization: declining trade barriers (GATT, WTO), technological change (containers, jets, telecommunications, internet), capital market liberalization, political shifts (end of Cold War, China opening, India reforms)
Measures of globalization: trade/GDP ratio, foreign direct investment/GDP ratio, cross-border capital flows, global value chain participation, KOF globalization index
Hyperglobalization vs slowbalization: 1990-2008 rapid integration era; 2008-present slower pace, peak trade/GDP, reshoring trends
Political trilemma (Rodrik): deep economic integration, nation-state sovereignty, and democratic politics - you can have any two but not all three
Benefits of globalization: poverty reduction (1 billion+ lifted out of extreme poverty), consumer variety, comparative advantage gains, technological diffusion, peace dividend
Costs of globalization: displaced workers in advanced economies, inequality widening within countries, race-to-the-bottom pressures, environmental externalities, loss of policy autonomy
Geopolitical backlash: US-China decoupling, sanctions on Russia, export controls on semiconductors, friend-shoring and nearshoring, industrial policy return
Supply chain fragility: COVID-19 exposed just-in-time vulnerabilities; Suez Canal blockage 2021; Red Sea attacks 2024; firms now pursuing redundancy over pure efficiency
lecture_notes.demonstration: Map the Apple iPhone global value chain to illustrate globalization of production. Design: Cupertino, California (Apple engineering). Critical components: A-series chip (design in California, manufacture by TSMC in Taiwan), memory (Samsung in South Korea, SK Hynix in Korea, Micron in Taiwan/Japan), display (Samsung/LG in Korea, BOE in China), cameras (Sony in Japan, LG in Korea), batteries (ATL in China, Sunwoda in China), accelerometers (Bosch in Germany), modems (Qualcomm in California, increasingly Apple in-house). Assembly: Foxconn in Zhengzhou, Pegatron in Shanghai, Luxshare in multiple Chinese sites, recently Tata in India and Foxconn in India and Vietnam. Logistics: DHL, FedEx, UPS, dedicated air freight for launch periods. Retail: Apple Stores in 30+ countries, third-party resellers, carrier partnerships. Discuss how this value chain was built over 20 years of globalization and how recent pressures (US-China tensions, India Make in India policy, pandemic risks) are now forcing diversification away from pure Chinese assembly toward India and Vietnam. Compare to an 'autarchic' alternative: what would it cost to make an iPhone entirely in the United States? Industry estimates range from $2,000-$3,500 per device, vs current ~$400 manufacturing cost, because the specialized supply chains, tooling, and labor simply do not exist domestically at scale.
setup_requirements: World Bank and WTO trade statistics 1960-2024
iPhone supply chain map (updated from various sources)
Rodrik The Globalization Paradox Ch. 1-2 excerpt

review.value: The assumption through the 1990s-2000s was that globalization would continue indefinitely. History shows otherwise - the first era of globalization (1870-1914) ended abruptly with World War I and did not recover until decades later. The current era peaked around 2008 and is now in partial retreat. Strategists must plan for both continued integration and potential fragmentation

Globalization has two distinct dimensions. Market globalization (selling the same product worldwide) has advanced but unevenly. Production globalization (making products through multi-country value chains) is now in retreat as firms prioritize resilience over pure efficiency. The two dimensions can move in different directions

The tension between deep integration, democratic politics, and national sovereignty is now active politics. Brexit, Trump, Meloni, Le Pen, Orban, and others have mobilized popular opposition to the losses globalization imposed on specific groups. Addressing these losses (through domestic policy) is now required for globalization to continue

review.key: Benefits and Costs Are Both Real
review.value: Globalization lifted over 1 billion people out of extreme poverty - the largest economic improvement in human history. It also created significant inequality within advanced economies, hollowed out manufacturing regions, and created supply chain fragility. Serious analysis must acknowledge both, not celebrate one and ignore the other

class_schedule.topic: National Differences: Political, Economic, and Legal Systems
learning_objectives: Classify countries by political system (democratic, totalitarian, hybrid) and implications for business
Distinguish economic systems: market, command, and mixed economies
Identify key legal system families (common law, civil law, theocratic, customary) and business implications
Evaluate country risk using political, economic, and legal dimensions
readings: Hill, International Business (13th ed.), Ch. 2 - National Differences in Political Economy
Acemoglu & Robinson, Why Nations Fail (2012), Ch. 1-3 - Institutions and Prosperity
North, Institutions, Institutional Change and Economic Performance (1990), Ch. 1-3
La Porta et al., 'Legal Determinants of External Finance,' Journal of Finance (1997)

lecture_notes.introduction: Countries differ profoundly in their political systems, economic systems, and legal systems, and these differences shape the conditions for business operations. A firm entering Germany encounters strong rule of law, efficient courts, transparent regulation, and predictable enforcement - conditions that make contracts reliable and investment safe. The same firm entering Russia encounters arbitrary regulatory decisions, unpredictable courts, politicized enforcement, and the risk that assets can be seized at government whim. The same firm entering Saudi Arabia encounters a legal system partly based on Sharia, with different rules for contracts, property, and business relationships. These national differences are not peripheral curiosities; they are first-order determinants of where business succeeds or fails. Daron Acemoglu and James Robinson's work on institutions (culminating in Why Nations Fail, 2012) argues that national prosperity is fundamentally determined by whether institutions are 'inclusive' (broadly distributed economic opportunity protected by rule of law) or 'extractive' (concentrated in elites who use political power to extract wealth). Inclusive institutions create prosperity; extractive institutions trap countries in poverty regardless of their natural resources or human capital. This session teaches you to analyze countries along three dimensions - political, economic, and legal - and to evaluate the business implications of national differences. We will examine concrete country cases: Germany (inclusive institutions producing high prosperity), Russia (extractive institutions producing moderate prosperity but fragile), China (hybrid system with strong state capacity), Nigeria (weak institutions despite resource wealth), and Singapore (strong institutions despite small size). Understanding these differences is essential for any decision about where to operate, where to invest, and how to structure cross-border operations.
key_concepts: Political system dimensions: collectivism vs individualism, democratic vs totalitarian, degree of political stability, corruption levels, protections for civil rights
Democracy types: parliamentary (UK, Germany), presidential (US, Brazil, France), hybrid (Russia, Turkey), illiberal democracies (Hungary, Poland)

Totalitarian regimes: communist (historical USSR, current North Korea), theocratic (Iran, historical Afghanistan), tribal (some African states), right-wing (historical Latin America), single-party state capitalism (China)
Economic systems: market economy (US, UK, most of Europe), command economy (North Korea, Cuba), mixed economy (France, India, most of Europe), state capitalism (China, Russia, Saudi Arabia)

Legal system families: common law (UK, US, Australia, India - precedent-based), civil law (France, Germany, Japan, most of Latin America - code-based), theocratic (Iran, Saudi Arabia - religious law), socialist (historical China - state interest), customary (many African nations - tradition-based)
Property rights protection: the ability of owners to use, benefit from, and transfer property; varies enormously across countries and is a major determinant of investment
Corruption: Transparency International Corruption Perceptions Index; ranges from Denmark (rank 1) to Somalia (rank 180); affects cost of doing business, regulatory risk, and ethical exposure
Intellectual property protection: enforcement of patents, copyrights, trademarks, trade secrets; major concern for IP-dependent industries entering weak-enforcement markets
Regulatory quality and stability: predictability of rules affecting business; WEF Global Competitiveness Report and World Bank Doing Business indicators measure these

Inclusive vs extractive institutions (Acemoglu-Robinson): inclusive institutions broadly distribute political and economic rights; extractive institutions concentrate them - a fundamental driver of long-run prosperity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Country comparison exercise: Germany, Russia, China, and Nigeria. Political: Germany (stable democracy, multi-party parliamentary system, very low corruption); Russia (hybrid regime, authoritarian trend since 2000, high corruption, politicized enforcement); China (single-party state with strong administrative capacity, no political opposition, moderate corruption but subject to campaigns); Nigeria (unstable democracy, periodic military interventions historically, very high corruption, regional tensions). Economic: Germany (social market economy, strong manufacturing, export-oriented, EU member with strong property rights); Russia (state capitalism with strategic sectors under Kremlin control, energy-dependent, sanctions since 2022); China (state-led market economy, hybrid public-private, world's factory, Party control over finance and strategic sectors); Nigeria (oil-dependent, underdeveloped manufacturing, large informal sector). Legal: Germany (civil law, strong courts, predictable enforcement, EU commercial law); Russia (civil law nominally but politicized enforcement, Yukos case, foreign investor assets seizure risk); China (civil law tradition with Party influence, improving but inconsistent enforcement, foreign IP at risk); Nigeria (common law due to British colonial legacy, courts slow, enforcement weak). Implications for business: Germany is attractive for long-term capital investment with low risk. Russia is problematic - returns may be high but risk of asset loss is real. China offers massive market access but comes with specific risks including technology transfer pressure and regulatory uncertainty. Nigeria offers access to Africa's largest market but with operational challenges.
setup_requirements: Country profiles from CIA World Factbook, World Bank, EIU Country Reports

review.value: Long-run economic outcomes depend more on institutions than on natural resources, geography, or culture. South Korea and North Korea were roughly equivalent in 1953; 70 years later South Korea is a rich democracy and North Korea is a desperately poor dictatorship. The difference is institutions, not geography or ethnicity. Any business analysis must take institutions seriously

Common law countries tend to have more developed financial markets, better investor protections, and more flexible contract enforcement than civil law countries. The LLSV research (La Porta et al.) documented this empirically. Firms operating across legal families must understand the different rules and protections in each

review.key: Rule of Law Is Not Optional
review.value: Firms need predictable enforcement of contracts, property rights, and regulations to invest long-term. Countries without rule of law can sometimes deliver short-term growth (China 1978-2010 is the most dramatic case) but struggle with long-term sustainable prosperity. Rule of law is as important as roads and electricity

Corruption is not a victimless inconvenience. It raises the cost of doing business, distorts resource allocation, undermines trust, and creates legal exposure under FCPA and similar anti-bribery laws. Firms operating in high-corruption environments need compliance systems that add cost and complexity

class_schedule.topic: Cultural Differences: Hofstede, GLOBE, and Cross-Cultural Management
learning_objectives: Apply Hofstede's six cultural dimensions to analyze business implications across countries
Distinguish Hofstede from GLOBE and Hall's high/low context framework
Identify common cross-cultural management failures and how to avoid them
Design culturally appropriate management practices for multinational teams
readings: Hill, International Business (13th ed.), Ch. 4 - Differences in Culture
Hofstede, Culture's Consequences (2nd ed., 2001), selected chapters
House et al., Culture, Leadership, and Organizations: The GLOBE Study of 62 Societies (2004)
Hall, Beyond Culture (1976), Ch. 7 - Context and Meaning

lecture_notes.introduction: Culture shapes nearly every aspect of how business is done: how negotiations proceed, how authority is exercised, how decisions are made, how feedback is given, how teams collaborate, how conflict is handled, and how trust is built. Managers who assume that their home-country management style will work abroad fail predictably and expensively. Walmart's failure in Germany (2006 exit after losing $1 billion) was substantially a culture failure: Walmart imposed American retail practices (morning cheers, smiling greeters, employee hand-holding before shifts) that German workers found bizarre and intrusive. Disney's early Euro Disney struggles (1992-1995) included cultural missteps: Disney imposed American service norms, American food offerings, and American park design without adapting to European customer preferences and worker expectations. These were not failures of analytical capability or capital; they were failures of cultural understanding. Geert Hofstede's landmark research at IBM in the 1970s and 1980s (surveying 117,000 employees in 40+ countries) produced the most influential framework for understanding national cultural differences: four original dimensions (power distance, individualism, masculinity, uncertainty avoidance) later extended to six (adding long-term orientation and indulgence). The GLOBE study (House et al., 2004) produced an alternative framework with nine dimensions based on a larger cross-cultural sample of managers. Edward Hall's anthropological work on high-context vs low-context communication provides another lens: in high-context cultures (Japan, China, Arab states), much of the meaning is implicit in relationships and situation; in low-context cultures (US, Germany, Netherlands), meaning is explicit in words. This session teaches you the major cultural frameworks and how to apply them to real management decisions.
key_concepts: Hofstede's Power Distance: the extent to which less powerful members of society accept unequal power distribution; high (Mexico, India, China) vs low (Denmark, Sweden, New Zealand)

Hofstede's Individualism vs Collectivism: whether people are expected to look after themselves (US, UK, Australia - highly individualist) vs integrated into cohesive groups (Japan, Korea, Venezuela - collectivist)
Hofstede's Masculinity vs Femininity: competition, achievement, success (masculine - Japan, Austria) vs cooperation, quality of life, caring for others (feminine - Sweden, Norway)
Hofstede's Uncertainty Avoidance: tolerance for ambiguity; high (Greece, Japan, France) vs low (Singapore, Denmark, Jamaica)
Hofstede's Long-Term Orientation: focus on future rewards, perseverance, thrift (China, Japan, Korea high) vs short-term (Nigeria, Ghana low)
Hofstede's Indulgence vs Restraint: gratification of basic desires related to enjoyment vs restraint by strict social norms

GLOBE dimensions: performance orientation, assertiveness, future orientation, humane orientation, institutional collectivism, in-group collectivism, gender egalitarianism, power distance, uncertainty avoidance

Hall's high-context vs low-context communication: high-context relies on implicit cues, relationships, and shared understanding (Japan, Arab states); low-context relies on explicit verbal messages (Germany, US)

Hall's monochronic vs polychronic time: monochronic (one thing at a time, punctuality, schedules - Germany, Switzerland) vs polychronic (multiple things simultaneously, flexible scheduling - Mexico, Saudi Arabia)
Cultural intelligence (CQ): individual ability to function effectively across cultures; includes cognitive (knowledge), metacognitive (awareness), motivational, and behavioral dimensions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply Hofstede to a specific management decision: designing a performance review system for a multinational team with members in the US, Germany, Japan, and India. US: low power distance, high individualism - direct feedback, individual ratings, performance differentiation expected. Germany: low power distance, individualist but also rule-oriented - structured feedback, explicit criteria, less personal touch than US. Japan: medium power distance, collectivist, high uncertainty avoidance - feedback must be indirect to preserve face, group harmony matters, individual ranking can be counterproductive, consensus decision-making. India: high power distance, collectivist, respect for hierarchy - feedback typically downward from supervisor, expectations of supervisor guidance, employees may not volunteer criticism upward. Implications: a uniform performance review system will not work. Either allow cultural adaptation in each country (consistent criteria, different delivery methods) or accept that the system will be effective in some countries and counterproductive in others. Then discuss a specific cross-cultural failure: Walmart in Germany, where American 'happy retail' practices offended German workers, morning chants were seen as cult-like, hand-holding before shifts was considered intrusive and patronizing, and the expected female-friendly 'greeter' role conflicted with German labor practices. Walmart exited Germany in 2006 after losing over $1 billion, selling to Metro AG.

review.value: Hofstede's dimensions describe national averages, not individual predictions. Two Germans will differ from each other more than the average German differs from the average American on many dimensions. Use frameworks to set expectations and ask questions, not to make individual judgments

Hofstede, GLOBE, and Hall each capture different aspects of cross-cultural variation. Sophisticated analysis uses multiple frameworks rather than relying on one. Hofstede is best for values, Hall is best for communication style, GLOBE adds distinctions Hofstede missed

Some management practices adapt easily (greetings, meeting formats, food offerings). Others are non-negotiable (safety standards, financial controls, ethics). The skill is knowing which is which. Cultural adaptation should not mean abandoning core values or standards

Cross-cultural effectiveness is a skill that improves with explicit training, immersion, and reflection. Firms that take CQ seriously (through pre-departure training, mentors, structured debriefs) produce expats who succeed; firms that assume cultural savvy is innate produce expensive failures

class_schedule.topic: International Trade Theory: From Absolute Advantage to New Trade Theory
learning_objectives: Explain Adam Smith's absolute advantage and David Ricardo's comparative advantage
Apply Heckscher-Ohlin theory to factor endowments and trade patterns
Distinguish new trade theory from classical trade theory using economies of scale
Apply Porter's diamond model of national competitive advantage
readings: Hill, International Business (13th ed.), Ch. 6 - International Trade Theory
Krugman, Obstfeld & Melitz, International Economics (11th ed.), Ch. 3-6
Smith, The Wealth of Nations (1776), Book IV Ch. 2 (excerpt)
Ricardo, On the Principles of Political Economy and Taxation (1817), Ch. 7 (excerpt)
Porter, The Competitive Advantage of Nations (1990), Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Why do countries trade with each other? Why does the US buy electronics from China and China buy soybeans from the US? Why does Germany export cars to Japan while Japan exports cars to Germany? Why do some small countries (Taiwan, Switzerland, Singapore) become major exporters while some resource-rich countries (Nigeria, Venezuela) remain stuck in poverty? International trade theory answers these questions, and the answers have evolved over 250 years. Adam Smith (1776) argued that countries benefit from specializing in what they produce most efficiently (absolute advantage) and trading for other goods. David Ricardo (1817) showed that even countries with no absolute advantage can benefit from trade by specializing in their RELATIVELY most efficient products (comparative advantage). This is arguably the most important and counterintuitive insight in economics: even a country that is worse at everything than its trading partners can still benefit from trade. Heckscher-Ohlin (1919-1933) extended the theory by explaining what determines comparative advantage: countries export goods that intensively use their abundant factors of production. Labor-abundant countries export labor-intensive goods; capital-abundant countries export capital-intensive goods. Paul Krugman's 'new trade theory' (1979-1991, Nobel 2008) added a crucial layer: economies of scale and imperfect competition can create trade patterns that classical theory does not predict. Why do Germany and Japan both export cars to each other? Not because of comparative advantage, but because consumers value variety and producers benefit from scale. Michael Porter's The Competitive Advantage of Nations (1990) offered yet another layer: the 'diamond' of factor conditions, demand conditions, related and supporting industries, and firm strategy/structure/rivalry explains why specific industry clusters (Italian leather, Swiss watches, Hollywood film, Silicon Valley tech) emerge in specific national contexts. Each theory is partial; together they offer a comprehensive explanation of international trade patterns.
key_concepts: Absolute advantage (Smith, 1776): a country has absolute advantage when it can produce a good using fewer inputs than any other country; free trade allows each country to specialize in its absolute advantages

Comparative advantage (Ricardo, 1817): a country has comparative advantage when it produces a good at lower opportunity cost than other countries; even with no absolute advantage, countries benefit from trade via comparative advantage
Opportunity cost: what is given up to produce a good; the basis of comparative advantage; a country should produce what it sacrifices least by producing
Heckscher-Ohlin theory: trade patterns reflect factor endowments - countries export goods that intensively use their abundant factors (labor-abundant countries export labor-intensive goods)
Stolper-Samuelson theorem: trade affects different factors differently - trade raises the return to abundant factors and lowers the return to scarce factors; explains political conflict over trade
Leontief paradox: the US, despite being capital-abundant, exported labor-intensive goods - an empirical puzzle for Heckscher-Ohlin; resolved by distinguishing types of labor (skilled vs unskilled)

Product life cycle theory (Vernon, 1966): goods are first produced in advanced countries, then production shifts to developing countries as products standardize - describes historical patterns but less relevant in the global value chain era
New trade theory (Krugman): economies of scale and imperfect competition can create trade patterns where countries export and import similar goods; explains much intra-industry trade
First-mover advantage in trade: firms and countries that achieve scale first in an industry can sustain dominance (Boeing in commercial aircraft, TSMC in advanced chips)

Porter's diamond: factor conditions, demand conditions, related and supporting industries, firm strategy/structure/rivalry - four elements that explain why particular countries produce world-leading firms in specific industries
lecture_notes.demonstration: Work through Ricardo's comparative advantage with a concrete numerical example. Assume Portugal and England each have 1,000 hours of labor. Portugal can produce 1 unit of wine per 10 hours or 1 unit of cloth per 20 hours. England can produce 1 unit of wine per 30 hours or 1 unit of cloth per 15 hours. Portugal has absolute advantage in both goods (wine: 10 vs 30; cloth: 20 vs 15). But comparative advantage differs: in Portugal, 1 wine costs 0.5 cloth (20/10 hours ratio); in England, 1 wine costs 2 cloth (15/30 hours ratio). Portugal has comparative advantage in wine (lower opportunity cost); England has comparative advantage in cloth. If both countries specialize in their comparative advantage and trade, total output increases. Without trade: Portugal produces 50 wine + 25 cloth; England produces 16.67 wine + 33.33 cloth. With specialization: Portugal produces 100 wine (all labor on wine); England produces 66.67 cloth. Total: 100 wine + 66.67 cloth (vs. 66.67 wine + 58.33 cloth without trade). Both countries can consume more by specializing and trading. This is the fundamental insight: trade is not a zero-sum game where one country's gain is another's loss; it is positive-sum, with both countries better off. Then apply new trade theory to car trade between Germany and Japan: both export cars to each other because consumers want variety (BMW, Mercedes, VW vs Toyota, Honda, Nissan) and producers benefit from scale. Classical trade theory cannot explain this; new trade theory can.
setup_requirements: Trade data: WTO World Trade Statistical Review

review.key: Comparative Advantage Is Counterintuitive But Right
review.value: The insight that a country with no absolute advantage can still benefit from trade through comparative advantage is one of the most important results in economics. It is also one of the most frequently misunderstood. Every trade economist agrees; popular discussions often conflate absolute and comparative advantage

Trade is positive-sum overall but creates distributional losers within countries. Stolper-Samuelson explains why: trade raises returns to abundant factors and lowers returns to scarce factors. Manufacturing workers in advanced countries have been hurt by trade with low-wage countries; capital owners have benefited. Addressing the losers is a political necessity if trade is to remain viable

No single theory explains all trade. Classical theory (Ricardo, Heckscher-Ohlin) explains inter-industry trade between countries with different factor endowments. New trade theory (Krugman) explains intra-industry trade between similar countries. Porter's diamond explains why specific industries cluster in specific nations. Use the right theory for the pattern you are analyzing

Tariffs, subsidies, and trade agreements affect who gains and who loses from trade. The 2018-2025 US-China trade war is a large-scale experiment in trade policy effects: tariffs raised US consumer prices, reduced Chinese exports to the US, encouraged relocation of supply chains to Vietnam and Mexico, and had modest effects on overall US employment in the targeted industries

class_schedule.topic: Foreign Direct Investment: Theory and Practice
learning_objectives: Explain theories of foreign direct investment: internalization, eclectic (OLI), and follow-the-leader
Distinguish horizontal from vertical FDI and identify the strategic logic of each
Analyze FDI flows globally and identify the major source and destination countries
Evaluate the costs and benefits of inward and outward FDI from host and home country perspectives
readings: Hill, International Business (13th ed.), Ch. 8 - Foreign Direct Investment
Dunning, 'The Eclectic (OLI) Paradigm of International Production,' International Business Review (2000)
Buckley & Casson, The Future of the Multinational Enterprise (1976), Ch. 2
UNCTAD, World Investment Report (2024), selected chapters

lecture_notes.introduction: Foreign direct investment (FDI) is the economic activity where a firm owns and controls productive assets in another country. Unlike portfolio investment (buying foreign stocks or bonds), FDI involves taking operating control: building factories, acquiring subsidiaries, establishing sales offices, creating research centers. FDI is central to contemporary globalization - the stock of global FDI exceeded $45 trillion in 2023, and multinational corporations' foreign affiliates generated over $33 trillion in revenue, roughly one-third of global output. Understanding why firms invest abroad, how they choose entry modes, and what the effects are on home and host countries is essential for any global strategy. The basic puzzle is this: why would a firm choose to invest in building operations in a foreign country when it could instead license technology to a local firm or export from its home country? The three options - exporting, licensing, and FDI - each have tradeoffs. Exporting avoids the risks and costs of foreign operations but faces tariffs, transportation costs, and currency risks. Licensing earns royalty income without direct investment but risks technology leakage and gives up control. FDI provides direct control and potentially higher returns but requires capital, management attention, and exposure to foreign political and economic risk. The theories of FDI explain when each mode is preferred. John Dunning's eclectic paradigm (or OLI framework) organizes the analysis: FDI is preferred when firms have Ownership advantages (proprietary technology, brand, management capability), Location advantages (market access, low-cost inputs, resources), and Internalization advantages (reasons to keep the capability in-house rather than licensing). When all three conditions are present, FDI is the optimal mode. This session teaches the theories and applies them to real multinational investment decisions.
key_concepts: Foreign direct investment (FDI): ownership and control of productive assets in another country; distinguished from portfolio investment by the control dimension
Horizontal FDI: replication of home-country operations in another country to serve that local market (Toyota building factories in the US to sell to US customers)
Vertical FDI: forward (marketing or distribution abroad) or backward (raw materials or components abroad) integration in the production chain
Platform FDI: investment in a country to serve regional or global markets, not the local market (Intel fabs in Ireland serving Europe)
OLI paradigm (Dunning): FDI occurs when a firm has Ownership advantages (firm-specific), Location advantages (country-specific), and Internalization advantages (transaction-specific)
Internalization theory (Buckley & Casson): firms internalize activities (do them in-house) when market transactions are inefficient due to asset specificity, uncertainty, or opportunism
Follow-the-leader theory (Knickerbocker): firms in oligopolistic industries invest abroad in response to competitors to prevent rivals from gaining exclusive access to foreign markets
Product life cycle FDI (Vernon): new products are first produced in the home country; as products standardize, production shifts to lower-cost locations
Greenfield vs M&A FDI: greenfield builds new operations; M&A acquires existing operations; tradeoffs involve speed, cost, integration challenges, and existing capability
FDI flows: UNCTAD reports annually; top source countries historically US, UK, Japan, Germany; top destination countries US, China, UK, Hong Kong; recent shifts toward India, Southeast Asia
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply the OLI framework to Toyota's decision to build vehicles in the US. Ownership advantages: Toyota Production System (TPS), quality reputation, engineering capability, brand loyalty - all firm-specific advantages Toyota can deploy anywhere. Location advantages: access to the world's largest car market, avoiding tariffs and political pressure, lower transportation costs than shipping from Japan, currency risk management, labor costs competitive once local wages factored in, political goodwill from US job creation. Internalization advantages: Toyota needs to maintain TPS quality, which requires hands-on management, culture training, and control that licensing to US manufacturers would not provide. Historical note: Toyota's first US venture was NUMMI (1984), a joint venture with GM in California, which was partially an experiment to test whether TPS could transfer to US workers. The success of NUMMI led Toyota to build fully-owned plants in Kentucky, Indiana, Texas, and Mississippi. By 2024, Toyota produced more vehicles in the US than it imported. The OLI framework explains each decision: Ownership (TPS, brand), Location (US market access, trade policy pressure), Internalization (TPS cannot be effectively licensed - it requires direct operational control). Contrast with Toyota's decision NOT to manufacture in every country where it sells - Toyota exports to many smaller markets because location advantages do not justify FDI costs at lower sales volumes.
setup_requirements: Toyota US manufacturing history and production data

review.key: FDI Is Not Just About Market Access
review.value: Firms invest abroad for multiple reasons: market access, resources, cost efficiency, strategic positioning, risk reduction, and circumvention of trade barriers. A mature global strategy considers all motivations and matches them to location decisions

Dunning's OLI framework is the standard tool for evaluating FDI decisions. It forces explicit analysis of firm-specific advantages, country-specific attractions, and reasons to integrate rather than contract. When all three conditions are present, FDI is preferred; when any is missing, alternative modes should be considered

Greenfield vs M&A is a major strategic decision. Greenfield is slower but allows building the exact capability needed; M&A is faster but requires integrating different culture, systems, and people. The best choice depends on timing, available targets, capability needs, and home-country management capacity

review.key: FDI Is Political as Well as Economic
review.value: Governments actively seek to attract (or repel) foreign investment through tax incentives, regulatory treatment, and political signals. FDI decisions increasingly involve political risk analysis: the 2018-2025 scrutiny of Chinese FDI in the US, export controls on technology transfer, and national security reviews have become first-order considerations

class_schedule.topic: Exchange Rates and the International Monetary System
learning_objectives: Explain how exchange rates are determined through purchasing power parity, interest rate parity, and Fisher effects
Distinguish fixed, floating, and managed exchange rate regimes
Analyze the major institutions of the international monetary system (IMF, World Bank, BIS)
Evaluate currency crises and their implications for firms
readings: Hill, International Business (13th ed.), Ch. 10 - The International Monetary System, Ch. 11 - The Foreign Exchange Market
Krugman, Obstfeld & Melitz, International Economics (11th ed.), Ch. 14-18
Eichengreen, Globalizing Capital (3rd ed., 2019), Ch. 1-3
Rogoff, 'The Purchasing Power Parity Puzzle,' Journal of Economic Literature (1996)

lecture_notes.introduction: Exchange rates are the prices at which one currency can be exchanged for another, and they are among the most consequential prices in the global economy. A firm that earns revenue in euros and pays expenses in dollars sees its profitability swing with the EUR/USD rate. A country whose currency appreciates sees its exports become less competitive. A country whose currency depreciates sees imports become more expensive, potentially fueling inflation. Exchange rate movements since the breakdown of Bretton Woods in 1973 have been large: the dollar-yen rate has ranged from 360 to 80 to 160 over this period. Understanding how exchange rates are determined and how they affect firms is essential for international business. Three foundational theories explain exchange rate movements. Purchasing power parity (PPP) argues that exchange rates should adjust so that identical baskets of goods cost the same across countries after currency conversion - the Big Mac Index from The Economist is a popular application. Interest rate parity (IRP) argues that exchange rate expectations should offset interest rate differentials across countries, preventing risk-free arbitrage. The international Fisher effect argues that expected inflation differentials should drive expected exchange rate changes. These theories work better in the long run than the short run - exchange rates can deviate from fundamentals for years due to speculation, central bank intervention, or capital flow dynamics. This session also covers the institutions of the international monetary system: the IMF (liquidity provider and crisis lender), the World Bank (development lender), the BIS (central bank coordinator), and the various regional arrangements. We will examine several currency crises (1997 Asian, 2001 Argentina, 2008 global, 2022 Turkey and Russia) to understand how exchange rate dynamics affect real economies and business operations.
key_concepts: Exchange rate: the price of one currency in terms of another; spot rates for immediate delivery, forward rates for future delivery

Purchasing power parity (PPP): identical goods should cost the same across countries after currency conversion; absolute PPP rarely holds, relative PPP suggests exchange rates adjust with inflation differentials
Interest rate parity (IRP): expected exchange rate changes should equal interest rate differentials; covered interest parity is a no-arbitrage condition that typically holds within small margins
International Fisher effect: nominal interest rate differentials should equal expected inflation differentials; used to forecast long-term exchange rate trends
Fixed exchange rate: government commits to maintaining a specific exchange rate through intervention (historical gold standard, Bretton Woods 1944-1971, current Hong Kong dollar peg)
Floating exchange rate: market forces determine the exchange rate (US dollar, euro, yen, British pound since 1973)
Managed (dirty) float: government intervenes periodically to influence the exchange rate without committing to a specific level (China yuan, many emerging market currencies)
Bretton Woods system (1944-1971): fixed exchange rates tied to the US dollar, which was convertible to gold at $35/oz; collapsed when US suspended gold convertibility in 1971
International Monetary Fund (IMF): lender of last resort to countries in currency crisis, monitors exchange rate policies, provides technical assistance
Currency crisis: a sudden large devaluation of a currency, often accompanied by capital flight, banking stress, and economic recession (1997 Asian crisis, 2001 Argentina, 2022 Turkey)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply PPP to the Big Mac Index and discuss why exchange rates deviate from PPP in the short run. In January 2024, a Big Mac costs $5.69 in the US, €4.75 in the Eurozone, £4.19 in the UK, ¥450 in Japan, and ¥22.50 in China. If PPP held, the exchange rates would be: EUR/USD = 0.83 (actual ~0.92), GBP/USD = 0.74 (actual ~0.79), USD/JPY = 79 (actual ~148), USD/CNY = 3.95 (actual ~7.1). All currencies except the Euro are 'undervalued' against the dollar by the Big Mac measure. The Japanese yen is 47% undervalued, suggesting either Japan is cheaper than fundamentals justify or the yen will appreciate. The Chinese yuan is 44% undervalued, consistent with the long-standing US claim of Chinese currency manipulation. These deviations can persist for decades because of productivity differentials (Balassa-Samuelson effect), trade barriers, non-tradable services (rent, labor), capital controls (in China's case), and market inefficiencies. Implications for firms: a US firm exporting to Japan sees its products cost less in yen than PPP would suggest, supporting export competitiveness. A Japanese firm exporting to the US sees its dollar prices much higher than PPP would suggest, eroding competitiveness. Then walk through the 1997 Asian financial crisis: Thailand maintained a dollar peg while running a large current account deficit and accumulating short-term dollar debt. When currency speculators bet against the baht in July 1997, Thailand had insufficient reserves to defend the peg. The baht fell 50% within weeks. Contagion spread to Malaysia, Indonesia, Philippines, Korea, with currency crashes, bank failures, and deep recessions. Firms with unhedged dollar debt suffered the worst: Korean chaebol had borrowed heavily in dollars to invest in won assets, and the sharp won depreciation made their debt unaffordable. Firms like Daewoo Motor went bankrupt. The crisis reshaped how emerging markets manage currencies and debt.
setup_requirements: Big Mac Index (The Economist, current data)

review.value: Exchange rates converge toward PPP over years to decades, but short-run movements are driven by capital flows, speculation, central bank intervention, and news shocks. Use PPP for long-term planning; do not use it for short-term hedging decisions

review.key: Currency Risk Is Real and Material
review.value: Firms with cross-border operations face transaction exposure (contract-level), translation exposure (financial reporting), and economic exposure (competitive position). Each requires different management approaches. Pretending currency risk will 'average out' is usually wrong

Fixed exchange rate regimes look stable until they break. Thailand 1997, Russia 1998, Argentina 2001 all had 'stable' fixed or pegged rates until speculators forced devaluation. Countries with fixed rates must hold enough reserves, maintain consistent macro policy, and limit external debt - or they will eventually face a crisis

Firms operating in emerging markets must consider local currency volatility as a strategic issue, not just a financial matter. Currency crises affect input costs, customer purchasing power, competitive dynamics, and debt service. Global strategy in emerging markets requires currency resilience, not just hedging

class_schedule.topic: Global Strategy: Multi-Domestic, Global, Transnational, and International
learning_objectives: Distinguish four global strategy types: international, multi-domestic, global, and transnational
Apply the integration-responsiveness framework (Bartlett & Ghoshal)
Identify which global strategy is appropriate for different industries and contexts
Analyze real-world examples of each strategy type
readings: Hill, International Business (13th ed.), Ch. 13 - The Strategy of International Business
Bartlett & Ghoshal, Managing Across Borders (2nd ed., 1998), Ch. 1-4
Ghemawat, 'Managing Differences: The Central Challenge of Global Strategy,' HBR (March 2007)
Prahalad & Doz, The Multinational Mission (1987), Ch. 1-2 - The IR Grid

lecture_notes.introduction: How should a multinational firm be organized globally? Two fundamental tensions shape the answer: the pressure to integrate operations globally for efficiency (common products, centralized production, global brand) versus the pressure to adapt to local market differences (language, taste, regulation, distribution). Different industries face these pressures differently. Semiconductors need massive scale and R&D, favoring global integration with standardized products worldwide. Food retail needs to match local tastes and dietary preferences, favoring local adaptation. Some industries (automobiles, consumer electronics) need both - efficient global production AND local customization. Christopher Bartlett and Sumantra Ghoshal's Managing Across Borders (1989, updated 1998) developed the most influential framework for classifying multinational strategies. They identified four types based on two dimensions: pressure for global integration and pressure for local responsiveness. International strategy (low on both): transfers capabilities from home country to foreign markets without significant adaptation (early Kodak, Procter & Gamble). Multi-domestic strategy (low integration, high responsiveness): operates each country as a standalone business with local strategy and local products (Unilever historically, Nestle in many product categories). Global strategy (high integration, low responsiveness): operates as one worldwide operation with standardized products and centralized decision-making (semiconductors, pharmaceuticals). Transnational strategy (high integration, high responsiveness): combines global efficiency with local responsiveness through a complex network organization (modern IBM, Procter & Gamble after restructuring). The transnational is theoretically the strongest but organizationally the most difficult. This session teaches you to diagnose which strategy fits different situations and to understand why most firms end up with hybrids that try to balance the tensions.
key_concepts: Integration-responsiveness framework (Prahalad & Doz, Bartlett & Ghoshal): two dimensions that characterize international business strategy challenges
Pressures for global integration: scale economies, R&D costs, global customers, standardized technology, universal needs, competition from global rivals
Pressures for local responsiveness: consumer taste differences, local regulation, distribution channels, cultural norms, host government pressures
International strategy: leverages core capabilities developed at home by transferring them abroad with minimal adaptation; headquarters retains most decision-making
Multi-domestic strategy: treats each country as an independent market with local strategy, local products, and local management; minimizes coordination but foregoes scale economies
Global strategy: operates as one worldwide business with standardized products, centralized production, and coordinated operations; maximizes scale economies but ignores local differences
Transnational strategy: combines global integration with local responsiveness through a complex network organization; capabilities flow in multiple directions, not just from headquarters outward
Organizational fit: each strategy type requires different organizational structures, decision rights, and management processes
Global standardization vs localization: key tradeoff affecting product design, marketing, distribution, and branding decisions
Aggregation-adaptation-arbitrage (AAA framework, Ghemawat): three distinct strategies for global success - aggregation (scale), adaptation (local fit), arbitrage (exploit national differences)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply the integration-responsiveness framework to four firms. 1. Intel: strong global strategy. Standardized semiconductors sold worldwide, centralized fabs in a few locations (Arizona, Israel, Ireland), global coordination of design and manufacturing. Local responsiveness is minimal - the same chip works everywhere. Integration pressure is extreme because chip design costs (billions per node) require global scale, and the product needs no localization. 2. McDonald's: transnational strategy. Global brand and operational standards (QSC: Quality, Service, Cleanliness) combined with significant local adaptation (McAloo Tikki burger in India with no beef; McSpicy in Singapore; McLobster in Maine; rice burgers in Asia; more extensive menus in Japan). The famous 'think global, act local' approach. 3. Unilever: historically multi-domestic but shifting toward transnational. Traditionally organized by country with local product portfolios (different detergents, different food products for each market). Recent restructuring under Paul Polman and Alan Jope pushed toward more global brands and global supply chains while retaining local adaptation for specific categories. 4. Tesla: approaching a global strategy. Standardized vehicles worldwide with minimal local adaptation, centralized engineering in California, uniform retail experience, single vehicle configuration per market. Contrast with traditional automakers (Ford, Toyota) who have historically designed different vehicles for different regions due to preference and regulatory differences. Discuss how these choices reflect industry pressures - semiconductors and cars have different integration-responsiveness needs.
setup_requirements: Company profiles: Intel, McDonald's, Unilever, Tesla global strategies

review.value: The right global strategy depends on the balance of integration and responsiveness pressures in the industry. Semiconductors cannot succeed with a multi-domestic strategy (scale is essential); food retail cannot succeed with a pure global strategy (local taste matters). Diagnose the pressures before picking the strategy

The transnational strategy - combining global efficiency with local responsiveness - is theoretically best but organizationally most difficult. It requires complex coordination, matrix structures, dual reporting, and mature management processes. Most firms fail to execute it well and end up sliding toward either global or multi-domestic

In a true transnational, capabilities are developed in subsidiaries and flow back to headquarters and other subsidiaries, not just outward from home country. P&G's Gillette shaving capability came partially from acquired global brands. Toyota learned flexibility from its Japanese operations and transferred it outward. Multi-directional learning is a transnational hallmark

A global strategy needs centralized decision-making; a multi-domestic needs decentralized authority; a transnational needs matrix organization with dual reporting. Mismatches between strategy and structure (e.g., a firm with global strategy but country-head autonomy) produce confusion and poor execution

class_schedule.topic: Entry Mode Strategies: Exporting to Wholly-Owned Subsidiaries
learning_objectives: Compare six entry modes: exporting, turnkey, licensing, franchising, joint ventures, wholly-owned subsidiaries
Analyze the control-risk-commitment tradeoffs of each mode
Apply entry mode analysis to specific firm and market decisions
Evaluate common entry mistakes and how to avoid them
readings: Hill, International Business (13th ed.), Ch. 15 - Entry Strategy and Strategic Alliances
Pan & Tse, 'The Hierarchical Model of Market Entry Modes,' Journal of International Business Studies (2000)
Anderson & Gatignon, 'Modes of Foreign Entry: A Transaction Cost Analysis,' Journal of International Business Studies (1986)
Kogut, 'Joint Ventures: Theoretical and Empirical Perspectives,' Strategic Management Journal (1988)

lecture_notes.introduction: When a firm decides to enter a new foreign market, it faces a fundamental choice: how to structure its market presence. The options range from simple exporting (shipping products from home country production) to wholly-owned subsidiaries (building or acquiring complete operations in the foreign market). Between these extremes are turnkey projects, licensing, franchising, joint ventures, and various hybrid arrangements. Each mode involves tradeoffs among control (ability to manage operations), risk (capital exposure and commitment), and commitment (organizational resources required). A firm that chooses the wrong entry mode can waste millions of dollars, damage relationships with partners, expose technology to theft, or lose market opportunities to rivals. The right entry mode depends on the firm's objectives, capabilities, risk tolerance, the target market's characteristics, and the nature of the industry. This session teaches the analytical frameworks for entry mode decisions and examines specific cases where firms chose wisely or poorly. Walmart's entry into Germany through acquisition of Wertkauf and Interspar was an acquisition strategy that failed due to cultural integration problems and German market characteristics. Starbucks' entry into Japan through joint venture with Sazaby was a JV strategy that succeeded because the Japanese partner provided local knowledge Starbucks needed. Tesla's 2019 entry into China through a wholly-owned subsidiary was unprecedented because China traditionally required JVs in automotive, but Tesla negotiated a unique arrangement that gave it full control. Each case illustrates how the right entry mode depends on specific circumstances - there is no universally correct answer.
key_concepts: Exporting: shipping products from home country to foreign market; low risk and commitment, but limited control over foreign distribution
Turnkey projects: contractor builds operational facility then turns it over to client; common in large infrastructure, industrial plants, oil refineries
Licensing: grants a foreign licensee the right to use IP (patents, trademarks, technology) in exchange for royalty; low risk but risks technology transfer and limits earnings
Franchising: licensor provides IP plus business format and ongoing support; franchisee operates the business under the licensor's rules; common in services (McDonald's, Marriott)
Joint ventures: two or more firms create a jointly-owned entity; combines complementary resources but complicates decision-making and risks partner conflict
Wholly-owned subsidiaries: full ownership of foreign operations; maximum control and profit capture but highest capital commitment and risk
Greenfield investment: build new operations from scratch in a foreign market; slower but allows exact capability design
Acquisition: buy existing foreign operations; faster market entry but requires integration and inherits existing capabilities (good or bad)
Entry mode choice factors: firm capabilities, target market characteristics, industry structure, strategic objectives, risk tolerance, legal requirements
Entry mode sequencing: firms often progress from low-commitment (exporting) to high-commitment (WOS) modes as they learn about markets and build capability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare three entry decisions and their outcomes. 1. Starbucks in Japan (1996): joint venture with Sazaby League, a Japanese retail company. Rationale: Japan was Starbucks' first international venture, Japanese market was complex and opaque to American retailers, Sazaby provided local real estate knowledge, cultural understanding, and regulatory navigation. Results: Starbucks Japan grew to over 1,800 locations, Starbucks eventually bought out Sazaby's stake in 2014. The JV served its purpose during entry phase. 2. Walmart in Germany (1997-2006): acquisition of Wertkauf (21 stores) and Interspar (74 stores). Rationale: Germany was Europe's largest retail market; acquisitions provided immediate scale. Results: disaster. Walmart's American management practices clashed with German labor laws and worker expectations; Walmart never understood German consumer preferences (Germans shop differently, value different things than Americans); existing stores were in poor locations and required massive investment; unions were hostile. Walmart lost over $1 billion and sold to Metro AG in 2006. 3. Tesla in China (2018-2019): wholly-owned subsidiary. Rationale: Tesla negotiated a historic deal with Shanghai government allowing 100% ownership without a Chinese JV partner (unprecedented in Chinese automotive), receiving tax benefits, land, and regulatory support. Chinese government wanted Tesla's EV technology to accelerate local industry. Tesla kept full control of intellectual property and operations. Results: Shanghai factory became Tesla's most productive and profitable, producing over 700,000 vehicles per year with Chinese labor at 65% the cost of US production. Analysis: each entry mode was appropriate to its circumstances. JV made sense for Starbucks because local knowledge was needed. Acquisition was wrong for Walmart because the acquired targets didn't fit its operating model. WOS was optimal for Tesla because preserving EV technology was critical and unusual regulatory access was available.
setup_requirements: Starbucks Japan, Walmart Germany, Tesla China case materials
Transaction cost economics primer applied to entry mode
Comparative entry mode success rates from academic research

review.key: Entry Mode Is Strategic, Not Just Tactical
review.value: The entry mode decision has consequences that last decades. A joint venture creates obligations to a partner and commits resources for years. An acquisition inherits existing capabilities and culture. A wholly-owned subsidiary requires building organizational capability. Treat entry mode as a core strategic choice, not a procurement decision

There is no universally best entry mode. The right choice depends on firm capabilities, market characteristics, industry structure, and strategic objectives. Frameworks like OLI and transaction cost economics help diagnose which factors matter most

Many firms progress from low-commitment (exporting) to higher commitment modes as they learn. This reduces risk by gaining market experience before committing capital. But staged entry is slower and can miss first-mover advantages in rapidly evolving markets

In joint ventures and alliances, the partner's capabilities, reliability, and strategic alignment are as important as the structural terms. Many alliances fail not because the structure is wrong but because the partners have different goals or cannot work together

class_schedule.topic: Global Production, Outsourcing, and Supply Chain Strategy
learning_objectives: Analyze global production strategies using the make-or-buy decision framework
Evaluate location decisions for global manufacturing and services
Identify supply chain resilience strategies for geopolitical risks
Analyze the shift from lowest-cost offshoring to friend-shoring and near-shoring
readings: Hill, International Business (13th ed.), Ch. 17 - Global Production and Supply Chain Management
Ferdows, 'Making the Most of Foreign Factories,' Harvard Business Review (1997)
Gereffi, Humphrey & Sturgeon, 'The Governance of Global Value Chains,' Review of International Political Economy (2005)
Shih, 'Bringing Manufacturing Back to the US Is Easier Said Than Done,' HBR Digital (April 2020)

lecture_notes.introduction: Where should a global firm make its products, and should it make them itself or buy them from suppliers? These are among the most consequential strategic decisions in international business, with implications for cost, quality, flexibility, innovation, risk, and capital intensity. For three decades - roughly 1990 to 2018 - the answer for most Western firms was 'offshore production to China and other low-cost Asian countries, outsource to specialized suppliers, optimize for lowest landed cost.' This strategy worked because China provided vast labor supply, competent manufacturing, improving quality, strong infrastructure, stable politics (until recently), and low trade barriers. Apple, Nike, Walmart, Dell, HP, and thousands of other firms built massive supply chains based on Chinese production. The consequences were dramatic: American and European manufacturing employment fell sharply, Asian manufacturing employment boomed, global trade volumes reached new highs, and consumer prices for manufactured goods stayed low despite rising wages in advanced economies. But the 2018-2026 period has brought a profound rethinking. The US-China trade war starting in 2018 imposed tariffs that disrupted the economics. COVID-19 in 2020-2021 exposed the fragility of single-source Chinese supply chains during disruption. US export controls on semiconductors (2022 and 2023) restricted technology access. Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022 showed how rapidly geopolitical events can destroy international business relationships. The 2022-2024 CHIPS Act, Inflation Reduction Act, and European equivalents poured hundreds of billions into domestic manufacturing subsidies. The result: firms are now diversifying production away from China toward India, Vietnam, Mexico, and back to the US and Europe. The strategic calculus has shifted from 'lowest cost' to 'resilience plus cost.' This session teaches you to analyze production strategy decisions in this changed environment.
key_concepts: Make-or-buy decision: whether to produce in-house or source from suppliers; determined by strategic importance, cost, capability, and risk
Location factors: labor cost, productivity, infrastructure, proximity to market, proximity to suppliers, political stability, currency risk, skill availability, regulatory environment

Ferdows factory roles: offshore (cost-efficient production), source (shift toward higher capability), server (local market), contributor (innovation), outpost (knowledge gathering), lead (full R&D and production)

Global value chains (GVC): multi-country production networks where different stages of production occur in different locations; Gereffi framework distinguishes market, modular, relational, captive, hierarchy governance types

Upstream vs downstream: upstream activities are closer to raw materials (mining, farming, R&D); downstream activities are closer to consumers (assembly, marketing, retail); value can accrue disproportionately at either end

Smile curve (Shih Jer-Yuan): value-added across the value chain forms a smile shape - high at upstream (R&D, design) and downstream (marketing, brand, retail), low in the middle (assembly, basic manufacturing)

Onshoring, offshoring, near-shoring, friend-shoring: reshoring to home country, offshoring to low-cost foreign locations, near-shoring to geographically close countries (Mexico for US, Poland for Germany), friend-shoring to geopolitically aligned countries
Supply chain resilience: capacity to respond to disruption through redundancy, diversification, visibility, flexibility, and strategic inventory; inverse to just-in-time optimization
Industrial policy: government measures to support specific industries (subsidies, tax breaks, tariffs, procurement preferences); CHIPS Act, IRA, EU Green Deal
Reshoring considerations: labor cost differences, automation reducing labor intensity, shipping costs, lead times, quality, intellectual property protection, political risk, incentives
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze Apple's China dependency and its diversification to India and Vietnam. As of 2020, over 95% of iPhone assembly occurred in China, with Foxconn's Zhengzhou plant alone producing 50% of the world's iPhones. This concentration was extremely efficient but created massive risks: US-China tensions threatened export access, COVID-19 lockdowns in 2022 disrupted production, labor unrest at the Zhengzhou plant in October 2022 caused Apple to miss Q4 shipment targets by millions of units, costing billions in revenue. Apple has been quietly diversifying: Wistron and Foxconn built iPhone assembly in India starting 2017; production in India grew from <1% of iPhones in 2020 to an estimated 10-14% by 2024, with Tata acquiring Wistron's India operations. Vietnam has become the center of Apple's AirPods, iPad, and Mac production. Costs are higher in India and Vietnam than in China, but Apple has decided to accept that premium for resilience. Key questions: how much can Apple realistically diversify? Chinese manufacturing has infrastructure, skill, and scale advantages that India and Vietnam cannot match in the short term. Apple's CEO Tim Cook has visited China repeatedly to maintain relationships and keep options open. The real target may be 30-40% diversification rather than full decoupling. Compare to Nike, which diversified earlier from China and now produces most footwear in Vietnam and Indonesia; Nike has roughly equivalent production costs and has successfully managed the transition. Discuss the lessons for any firm with concentrated Chinese production: start diversifying early, maintain dual sources, accept modest cost increases, invest in supplier capability development.
setup_requirements: Apple supply chain analysis and recent diversification data
CHIPS Act and Inflation Reduction Act industrial policy summaries

review.value: For three decades, firms optimized supply chains for cost with little attention to political risk. The 2018-2025 era has made geopolitics a first-order consideration. Any firm with significant China exposure must have a diversification plan and risk management process

Just-in-time supply chains maximize efficiency but minimize resilience. After COVID, firms are accepting higher inventory, dual sourcing, and strategic reserves to reduce disruption risk. The pendulum has swung from 'lean' to 'resilient,' with real cost implications

Reshoring manufacturing to home countries is often harder than politicians suggest. Decades of offshoring have eroded domestic supplier networks, skilled labor, and tacit manufacturing knowledge. Rebuilding requires years and massive investment. The CHIPS Act is helping, but even Apple cannot quickly move iPhone assembly to the US

review.key: Labor Cost Is Less Important Than It Was
review.value: As automation advances, direct labor becomes a smaller share of manufacturing cost. For many products, automation cost dominates labor cost, making high-wage locations competitive. This is reshaping the math of global production location decisions

learning_objectives: Apply the standardization vs. adaptation framework to global marketing decisions
Analyze global brand strategies and the tradeoffs of global vs local branding
Evaluate pricing strategies in international markets (market-based, cost-plus, transfer pricing)
Identify common international marketing failures and their causes
readings: Hill, International Business (13th ed.), Ch. 18 - Global Marketing and R&D
Keller, Strategic Brand Management (5th ed.), Ch. 12 - Managing Brands Over Geographic Boundaries
Levitt, 'The Globalization of Markets,' HBR (1983) - controversial claim about convergence
Quelch & Hoff, 'Customizing Global Marketing,' HBR (1986)

lecture_notes.introduction: Marketing across borders faces a fundamental question: how much should a firm standardize its marketing globally versus adapting to local markets? Standardization offers economies of scale (one global advertising campaign vs 50 national campaigns), consistency of brand image (Coca-Cola is red everywhere), and centralized decision-making. Adaptation offers relevance to local customers (different tastes, languages, cultures, purchasing power), avoidance of cultural offense, and responsiveness to local competition. Theodore Levitt famously argued in 1983 that 'the globalization of markets' was bringing convergence in consumer preferences, allowing firms to standardize aggressively. Subsequent experience has been more nuanced - some markets do converge (luxury goods, technology, youth fashion), while others remain stubbornly local (food, personal care, culturally-loaded products). The smartest global firms achieve 'glocalization' - maintaining a consistent global brand while adapting specific elements (product features, advertising, pricing, distribution) to local conditions. This session examines the key marketing decisions: product design (standardized or localized?), branding (global or local brand names?), pricing (uniform or market-based?), promotion (global campaigns or local creative?), and distribution (own the channel or work with local partners?). We'll examine both successes (Coca-Cola's global brand with local flavors, McDonald's global format with local menus) and failures (Procter & Gamble's Pert Plus shampoo launch in Japan where consumers preferred separate shampoo and conditioner, Ford's 'Pinto' being named 'small male genitalia' in Brazilian Portuguese).
key_concepts: Standardization vs adaptation: the central tradeoff in global marketing; standardization saves costs and preserves brand consistency, adaptation increases local relevance
4Ps adapted for global: Product (features, packaging), Price (local purchasing power), Place (distribution channels), Promotion (advertising, sales promotion); each can be standardized or localized
Global brand: a brand with consistent identity, positioning, and recognition across countries (Coca-Cola, Nike, Apple, BMW)
Local brand: a brand developed for and positioned to a specific country (Maggi in India, though now part of Nestle)
Glocalization: maintaining global brand consistency while adapting specific elements to local markets; 'think global, act local'
Country-of-origin effect: perceptions of products based on their country of origin; German engineering, Japanese electronics, Italian fashion, French cosmetics
Price discrimination across markets: charging different prices in different countries based on local willingness to pay, competition, and exchange rates
Gray market: unauthorized cross-border arbitrage when price differences exceed transportation costs; undermines local pricing strategies
Transfer pricing: prices charged between units of the same multinational firm in different countries; tax implications are significant and scrutinized
Distribution channel decisions: wholly-owned distribution, local distributors, e-commerce direct to consumer; tradeoffs in control vs speed vs local knowledge
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare three global marketing approaches. 1. Coca-Cola: strong global brand with substantial localization. Brand name, logo, red color, and iconic contour bottle are globally consistent. Advertising themes (happiness, sharing, refreshment) are globally consistent. But product specifications vary: Coke has different sweetness levels in different markets (sweeter in Asia, less sweet in Europe); product line differs (Georgia coffee in Japan, Inca Kola in Peru which Coca-Cola acquired); promotional activities are locally managed. Coca-Cola's 'glocal' approach balances global consistency with local relevance. 2. McDonald's: global format with local menu adaptation. QSC standards (Quality, Service, Cleanliness), store design, brand mark, and operational processes are globally consistent. But menu adapts extensively: no beef in India (McAloo Tikki, Maharaja Mac with chicken), rice-based meals in Asia, McLobster in Maine, McCafe coffee in Australia, vegetarian expansion in India, larger menus in Japan. McDonald's strategy works because the format is a valuable global asset but food preferences are culturally rooted. 3. Procter & Gamble: historically multi-domestic, now more glocal. Traditional P&G allowed country managers significant autonomy to tailor products and marketing. This produced successful local brands but inefficient use of global R&D and marketing investment. Under recent reorganization, P&G now has global brands (Tide, Pampers, Gillette) with more consistent positioning and shared marketing, while retaining local adaptation for specific categories. Discuss specific failures: Pepsi's 'Come Alive with the Pepsi Generation' translated in Chinese as 'Pepsi Brings Your Ancestors Back from the Dead'; Ford's 'Pinto' in Brazil; KFC's 'Finger Lickin' Good' in Chinese as 'Eat Your Fingers Off.' These are marketing failures of translation but illustrate broader cultural risks.
setup_requirements: Case studies of marketing failures and successes
P&G, Coca-Cola, McDonald's global marketing materials
Price comparison across markets for same products

review.key: Neither Pure Standardization Nor Pure Adaptation Works
review.value: Pure standardization ignores real differences that affect sales; pure adaptation loses the economies and consistency of global operations. The answer for most firms is selective adaptation - standardize where it matters less, adapt where it matters more. This requires analytical discipline about what matters for each product category

review.key: Brand Can Be Global While Product Is Local
review.value: A brand can maintain global identity (logo, color, positioning) while the actual product varies considerably across markets. Coca-Cola is recognizably 'Coke' everywhere but has different formulations. McDonald's is instantly recognizable everywhere but has radically different menus. Global brand + local product is often the right balance

Uniform global pricing fails because purchasing power and competition vary; local pricing creates gray market arbitrage and transfer pricing tax problems. Most firms use market-based pricing with enough consistency to avoid arbitrage but enough flexibility to reflect local conditions

Most famous international marketing failures are avoidable with basic cultural research: translation checks, local focus groups, cultural sensitivity reviews. Firms that skip these steps save hours but risk multi-million-dollar mistakes and brand damage

learning_objectives: Apply the ethnocentric, polycentric, geocentric, and regiocentric staffing frameworks
Design expatriate assignment programs including selection, training, compensation, and repatriation
Analyze cross-cultural training and its impact on expatriate success
Evaluate the management of local workforces in foreign operations
readings: Hill, International Business (13th ed.), Ch. 19 - Global Human Resource Management
Perlmutter, 'The Tortuous Evolution of the Multinational Corporation,' Columbia Journal of World Business (1969)
Black & Mendenhall, 'Cross-Cultural Training Effectiveness,' Academy of Management Review (1990)
Tung, The New Expatriates (1988), Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: How should a multinational firm staff its foreign operations? Should key positions be filled by home-country expatriates (parent country nationals, PCNs), local hires (host country nationals, HCNs), or talented third-country nationals (TCNs)? The answer affects knowledge transfer, local market responsiveness, costs, and career development. Howard Perlmutter's 1969 framework identified four orientations: ethnocentric (PCN staffing preferred), polycentric (HCN preferred), geocentric (best person regardless of nationality), and regiocentric (regional pools). Each has advantages and disadvantages. Ethnocentric staffing maintains strategic control and transfers home-country knowledge but costs more (expatriate packages are expensive) and can alienate local employees who see their career options capped. Polycentric staffing is cost-effective and ensures local knowledge but limits knowledge flow between headquarters and subsidiaries. Geocentric staffing is theoretically best (pick the best person regardless of nationality) but requires sophisticated global HR systems and international mobility. Most firms use hybrid approaches. Expatriate assignments remain central to multinational management despite their cost and difficulty. Research consistently finds that expatriate failure rates range from 16-40% (defined as early return or significant underperformance), with cultural adjustment problems being the leading cause. Better pre-departure training, family support, and repatriation planning substantially improve outcomes, but many firms underinvest in these areas. This session teaches the design of international staffing and expatriate programs using evidence from research and practice.
key_concepts: Ethnocentric staffing: key positions filled by parent country nationals (PCNs); maintains control, ensures knowledge transfer, but expensive and limits local career development
Polycentric staffing: host country nationals (HCNs) fill subsidiary management positions; cost-effective, ensures local knowledge, but limits knowledge flow between headquarters and subsidiaries
Geocentric staffing: best person regardless of nationality; optimizes capability and flexibility but requires sophisticated global HR systems and international mobility
Regiocentric staffing: regional talent pools within regions (EMEA, APAC, Americas); balances local knowledge with broader career paths
Parent Country Nationals (PCNs), Host Country Nationals (HCNs), Third Country Nationals (TCNs): three types of employees in multinational operations
Expatriate assignment lifecycle: selection, pre-departure training, ongoing support, performance management, repatriation
Expatriate failure: early return or significant underperformance; cultural adjustment, family issues, work role clarity are leading causes
