- Competition from local streamers (Hotstar in India, iQIYI in China, Globoplay in Brazil) requires stronger local positioning.
- Advertising tier expansion adds another layer of complexity to balance global ad sales with local market rates.

Comparison to alternatives:

If Netflix chose a global strategy (pure standardization): US/English content only, uniform pricing, minimal localization. Would likely stall in non-English markets and leave growth opportunity untapped. This was roughly Netflix's approach in 2010-2014 when it was primarily a US service.

If Netflix chose a multi-domestic strategy (pure localization): Separate country organizations, independent content strategies, minimal global coordination. Would lose the benefits of global content (Money Heist crossing from Spain to worldwide hit), global technology, and shared data analytics.

Netflix's actual approach - global platform and content strategy with significant local content investment and adaptation - is distinctively transnational and has been critical to its international success.

rubric.key: Clear description of all four strategies with examples
Correct identification of Netflix as transnational with evidence
Specific elements of global integration and local responsiveness

questions.question: Identify three reasons why firms are moving production from China to other countries (India, Vietnam, Mexico) in 2024-2026, and explain the tradeoffs.
questions.answer:
The reshoring and 'friend-shoring' of production away from China represents one of the most significant international business trends of the 2020s. Three main reasons drive this shift:

1. Geopolitical risk and tariff uncertainty.

The US-China trade relationship has deteriorated significantly since 2018. The Trump administration imposed tariffs on hundreds of billions of dollars of Chinese imports in 2018-2019; the Biden administration retained most of these tariffs and added new ones on strategic goods including semiconductors, solar panels, and electric vehicles in 2024. The 2025 Trump administration has raised tariffs further. Export controls on advanced semiconductors (October 2022 and tightened in 2023 and 2024) and investment screening restrictions have added non-tariff barriers.

Beyond trade policy, broader geopolitical concerns affect production decisions: the possibility of conflict over Taiwan, sanctions regimes that could apply to China in certain scenarios, Chinese government actions against foreign firms (Didi crackdown 2021, data security laws), and Chinese retaliatory actions (rare earth export controls, anti-sanctions laws).

Firms have concluded that concentrated Chinese production is a strategic vulnerability. Even firms that want to remain in China for market access are diversifying production away from China to reduce exposure. Apple's gradual shift of iPhone assembly to India and Vietnam is the most visible example, but thousands of firms are making similar decisions.

Tradeoffs: Diversification reduces geopolitical risk but costs money. Alternative locations have less developed infrastructure, supply chains, skilled labor, and tax incentives than China. Productivity and quality are typically lower initially. Moving faster increases risk of execution problems.

2. Supply chain resilience after COVID-19.

The COVID-19 pandemic exposed the fragility of just-in-time supply chains concentrated in single locations. When China's 'zero COVID' policy led to extended Shanghai lockdowns in Q1-Q2 2022, global supply chains suffered disruptions that caused shortages and delays worldwide. The Zhengzhou Foxconn plant (where 50% of the world's iPhones are assembled) had worker unrest in October 2022, forcing Apple to miss its Q4 shipment targets and lose billions in revenue. These disruptions demonstrated that extreme concentration creates single points of failure.

Firms have responded with 'China plus one' or 'China plus N' strategies - maintaining Chinese operations while developing capacity in other countries to provide alternatives during disruptions. The cost is higher than pure optimization, but the resilience is worth it to avoid catastrophic disruptions.

Tradeoffs: Multiple production locations require more management complexity, capital investment, and supplier development. But the insurance value of having alternatives is significant. Firms that maintained concentrated Chinese production through the 2022 disruptions lost market share to competitors that had diversified earlier.

3. Rising Chinese labor and operating costs.

Chinese manufacturing labor costs have risen steadily. Average manufacturing wages in China grew from $1-2/hour in 2000 to over $6/hour in 2020 and higher now. For labor-intensive industries (textiles, simple electronics assembly, low-value-added manufacturing), China is no longer the cheapest option. Vietnam, Bangladesh, Cambodia, and Indonesia offer lower labor costs.

Chinese energy costs, environmental compliance costs, and regulatory costs have also risen. Environmental protection has become more stringent (which is beneficial for Chinese air and water quality but adds cost to manufacturing). Recent policies on common prosperity and regulatory control of certain sectors have added uncertainty.

For firms that set up in China when labor costs were low, the original cost advantage has eroded. Diversifying to newer low-cost locations (or automating in higher-cost locations) may now be cheaper than maintaining Chinese production.

Tradeoffs: Cheaper labor in alternative locations often comes with lower productivity, less developed supply chains, and less skilled workforce. The total cost-per-unit calculation may or may not favor relocation, depending on the specific industry, automation level, and supply chain availability.

Additional drivers (worth noting):

4. Industrial policy in destination countries: Vietnam, India, Mexico, and others have actively competed to attract investment with tax incentives, streamlined approvals, and infrastructure support. Modi's 'Make in India' and Production Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes have successfully attracted iPhone assembly, electronics, and pharmaceuticals. Vietnam's long-term investment promotion has attracted Samsung, Intel, and many others.

5. Shorter supply chains and proximity to market: Near-shoring to Mexico (for US markets) and Turkey (for European markets) reduces shipping times and costs, enables just-in-time delivery to customers, and reduces currency risk. Tesla's decision to build in Mexico reflects this logic.

6. ESG and sustainability pressure: Chinese manufacturing has a higher carbon intensity than some alternatives due to coal-heavy electricity generation. Firms committed to reducing Scope 3 emissions may prefer locations with cleaner energy.

Limits and counter-trends:

Despite the shift, China remains dominant for many industries:
- China has massive scale, deep supplier networks, and tacit manufacturing knowledge that alternative locations cannot quickly replicate.
- China remains the largest or second-largest market for many firms; producing locally maintains market access.
- Diversification is slower and costlier than politicians suggest. Apple has been working on India production for over 5 years and has only reached ~14% of iPhone volume there.
- The 'China plus one' strategy typically maintains China as the largest production location while adding alternatives, not replacing China entirely.

Firms that moved out of China entirely (a small minority) have often struggled to replicate Chinese production efficiency. Most are pursuing gradual diversification while maintaining significant Chinese operations.

Implications for strategy: International business strategy now requires explicit analysis of geopolitical risk, supply chain resilience, and political economy - considerations that were secondary during the hyperglobalization era of 1990-2010. Firms that integrate these considerations into strategy will be better positioned than those that treat them as afterthoughts.

rubric.key: Three substantive reasons with specific mechanisms and examples
Honest tradeoff analysis for each reason
Recognition of limits and counter-trends

questions.question: A US consumer products company wants to launch its laundry detergent in Brazil. List four specific marketing adaptations it should consider and explain why each matters.
questions.answer:
Launching a US-developed product in Brazil requires analyzing Brazilian-specific factors in product, price, place, and promotion. Four specific adaptations to consider:

1. Product formulation adapted for Brazilian water and washing practices.

Brazilian water in many areas is 'hard' (high mineral content), which affects how detergent interacts with fabrics and cleaning performance. US detergents optimized for typical American water may underperform in Brazilian hard water. Additionally, Brazilian consumers often hand-wash clothes (particularly in lower-income households) or use front-loading washers with lower water temperatures than typical US machines.

Formulation adaptations needed:
- Water hardness compensation: add ingredients to soften water or improve performance in mineral-rich water.
- Cold-water effectiveness: optimize enzymes and surfactants for lower-temperature washing common in Brazil.
- Hand-washing performance: for brands targeting informal segments, formulate for hand-washing friction and skin gentleness.
- Stain types relevant to Brazilian climate: tropical climate and outdoor lifestyle means different stain profiles (sunscreen, sand, tropical food).

Local research (competitor analysis, focus groups, small-scale product testing) should validate formulation choices before national launch. Unilever and P&G both have extensive Brazilian R&D that addresses these factors.

2. Packaging and pricing tiered for Brazilian purchasing power.

Brazil has significant income inequality, with a large base-of-pyramid population alongside a middle and upper class. A single product format at a single price point will not serve the market effectively.

Adaptations:
- Multiple package sizes: small sachets or individual-use packets for BOP consumers who cannot afford a full bottle at once; medium-sized bottles for middle class; large economy sizes for upper middle class and larger households.
- Price points at appropriate levels: sachet at R$2-3, small bottle at R$8-10, large bottle at R$15-25 (example prices for 2024).
- Product tiering: premium formulation for upper market, standard for middle, simpler formulation for value segment. Different brand names may be appropriate to avoid cannibalization or brand confusion.

Hindustan Unilever's sachet strategy in India illustrates this principle - small packet sizes make the product affordable to consumers who could not afford full-size bottles. The same logic applies to Brazil's BOP segment.

3. Distribution through multiple channels matching Brazilian retail structure.

Brazilian retail has a distinct structure: large modern supermarkets (Carrefour, Pao de Acucar) in cities, pharmacy chains (Raia Drogasil) for daily convenience, neighborhood corner stores (padaria, mercearia) everywhere, and a large informal sector. Each channel requires different pricing, packaging, and distribution logistics.

Adaptations:
- Modern retail distribution: direct to Carrefour, Extra, Pao de Acucar with consumer-facing marketing support. Margin and listing fees typical of modern retail apply.
- Wholesale distribution: for small retailers, use local wholesalers who handle fragmented distribution to thousands of small stores. Relationship-based business.
- Direct sales networks: Natura's successful door-to-door/direct sales model in Brazil shows the power of relationship-based distribution. Laundry detergent may work through similar channels for certain segments.
- E-commerce: Mercado Livre (Amazon equivalent), Magazine Luiza, and newer platforms are growing. Product must be packaged for e-commerce shipping.
- Availability in multiple formats: the same product should appear in every channel relevant to the target consumer.

Coverage requires a distribution partner network rather than a single centralized system - something the US firm likely needs to build or acquire.

4. Marketing messages and media adapted for Brazilian culture.

Brazilian marketing differs from US marketing in several ways that affect laundry detergent:

- Cleanliness is culturally important: Brazilians generally emphasize appearance and cleanliness, which supports premium positioning for laundry care but also creates high expectations.
- Family and community values: Brazilian marketing often emphasizes family connections, maternal care, and community. Messages framing clean clothes as care for loved ones resonate better than purely functional messaging.
- Celebrity culture: Brazilians respond well to celebrity endorsements from telenovela stars, footballers, and local cultural figures - different from US celebrities.
- Humor and warmth: Brazilian advertising tends to be warmer and more emotional than typical US advertising, with humor and music featured prominently.
- Portuguese language: obviously translations needed, but also cultural adaptation - literal translations often fail because idioms, jokes, and cultural references do not transfer.
- Media mix: Brazilian consumers watch Globo's prime-time telenovelas, listen to radio widely in cars, and use WhatsApp and Instagram heavily. TikTok is growing. Traditional newspaper advertising is less important than in some markets.
- Regional differences: Brazil is regionally diverse - the Northeast, Southeast, and South have different cultural norms, dialects, and consumption patterns. National marketing may need regional adaptation or targeting.

Campaign example: a successful laundry detergent ad campaign in Brazil might feature a multi-generational family (grandmother, mother, child) with warm music, emphasize care and tradition, use regional dialect, and air prominently during Globo's 8pm novela time slot. A US ad featuring a clinical demonstration of stain-fighting would be less effective.

Additional adaptations (worth noting):

5. Retail pricing psychology: Brazilian consumers may respond differently to promotional pricing, bundle offers, and loyalty programs than US consumers.

6. Tax and regulatory compliance: ANVISA (Brazilian health regulator) must approve product formulations. Brazilian labeling laws are specific about ingredient disclosure and claims.

7. Competitive analysis: Local brands (Ype, OMO, Ariel Brazil) have entrenched positions. Competition from Natura (direct sales) and Hypera Pharma (broad consumer products) is intense. The US firm must position distinctively against these competitors.

8. Partnerships and local talent: hiring Brazilian marketing professionals, partnering with local agencies (Y&R Brazil, DM9, AlmapBBDO), and developing long-term presence is essential. Expat American marketing leaders without Brazilian experience will struggle.

Synthesis:

Successful entry into Brazil requires treating it as a distinct market with its own conditions, not a minor variant of the US market. Firms that invest in local research, local talent, local partnerships, and local adaptation succeed; firms that try to save costs by minimal adaptation typically fail. Unilever, P&G, and Colgate-Palmolive have built decades of Brazilian market knowledge and leading positions through deep adaptation. A new entrant should expect a multi-year investment to establish itself.

rubric.key: Four substantive marketing adaptations with specific reasoning
Examples of Brazilian-specific factors (language, distribution, media)
Recognition of competitive landscape and adaptation costs

questions.question: A US firm is sending an American manager on a 3-year assignment to run a new subsidiary in Japan. Describe the full expatriate assignment lifecycle including selection, preparation, compensation, and repatriation. What specific Japan-related considerations apply?
questions.answer:
Effective expatriate assignment management requires coordinated attention to selection, pre-departure preparation, in-country support, performance management, and repatriation planning. Japan presents specific considerations due to its high-context culture, unique business practices, and challenging living environment for Americans.

Selection (2-3 months before assignment):

Criteria for selection beyond technical competence:
- Cross-cultural competency: prior international experience, demonstrated adaptability, cultural curiosity, language aptitude
- Emotional resilience: ability to handle ambiguity, frustration, and isolation
- Family situation: spouse and dependents' willingness and ability to relocate; strong family support systems reduce failure rates
- Relationship skills: ability to build relationships with Japanese colleagues, subordinates, and customers
- Track record: success in home operations suggests (but does not guarantee) success abroad
- Health and fitness for the demands of relocation

Assessment methods: structured interviews with both the candidate and spouse/family, personality assessments (Big Five or similar), cross-cultural competency measures (Cultural Intelligence Scale), review of prior international experience.

Japan-specific selection considerations: Japanese business culture is hierarchical, consensus-based, relationship-driven, and high-context. Managers who thrive in direct American communication styles may struggle. Preference should be given to candidates who are patient, respectful of hierarchy, comfortable with ambiguity, and willing to learn Japanese customs. Prior exposure to Japan or East Asia is a significant advantage.

Pre-departure Preparation (2-3 months before relocation):

1. Cross-cultural training: 4-6 weeks of comprehensive Japan orientation covering:
- Japanese history, culture, and current events
- Business culture: meishi (business card) etiquette, ringi-sho consensus process, nemawashi informal consultation, wa (harmony), tatemae/honne (public face vs. true feelings), omoiyari (consideration for others)
- Language basics: at least 100 hours of Japanese study before departure, continuing in-country
- Negotiation styles: indirect communication, pause for consideration, avoiding direct 'no'
- Social etiquette: bowing, gift-giving, honorific language, seating arrangements, dining customs

2. Family preparation: spouse and dependents should receive parallel cultural training. Spouse employment is a major challenge in Japan due to work visa restrictions, language barriers, and social norms. Dependent children need international schooling guidance.

3. Practical preparation: housing reconnaissance trip (typically Tokyo costs $5,000-12,000/month for suitable expat housing), international school identification, banking arrangements, healthcare transition, personal legal matters.

4. Business context briefing: introduction to Japanese subsidiary leadership (ideally video calls before arrival), review of business goals, Japanese market conditions, competitive landscape, key relationships.

5. Language study: at minimum, basic Japanese for daily life (greetings, shopping, transportation). Business Japanese is ideal but takes years. Most expatriates rely on Japanese colleagues for language-intensive situations.

Compensation (determined before departure):

Balance Sheet Approach (standard for expat assignments):
- Home-country base salary maintained (e.g., $200,000 US)
- Cost of living allowance (COLA): Tokyo's cost index is approximately 115-125 vs. typical US cities - add $25,000-35,000 to compensate for higher prices
- Housing allowance: Tokyo housing is famously expensive for expat-quality apartments with Western amenities - $72,000-144,000/year ($6,000-12,000/month)
- Hardship or mobility premium: Japan is low-hardship but cultural adjustment is real - typically 10-15% of base salary ($20,000-30,000)
- Education allowance: international schools for dependents cost $25,000-35,000 per child per year
- Home leave: 1 trip per year for the family back to the US, fully funded
- Tax equalization: the company pays all Japanese taxes and the US tax, ensuring the expat pays no more or less total tax than they would in the US. This is essential because Japanese income taxes are high and the interplay with US taxes is complex.
- Relocation support: moving expenses, temporary housing, spouse employment assistance, language training allowance

Total package: for a $200,000 base-salary manager, the full expat package often totals $500,000-800,000 in the first year. The premium is significant but necessary.

In-country support and performance management (ongoing during assignment):

1. Onboarding: first 30 days focus on introductions, relationship building, and learning rather than driving immediate change. Senior Japanese colleague as mentor helps navigate cultural aspects.

2. Ongoing language and cultural education: continuing Japanese study, cultural coaching, regular reflection with cross-cultural mentor.

3. Dual reporting: expatriate typically reports to both Japanese business leader (for day-to-day operations) and home country executive (for strategic alignment and career management). Managing the dual reporting requires clear objectives and regular communication.

4. Performance management: objectives must account for Japan's business environment (longer decision cycles, relationship-building timelines, consensus requirements). US-style quarterly objective reviews may miss the slower Japanese pace. Performance evaluation should include cultural adaptation and relationship building, not just business results.

5. Social and community integration: expat community support (American Chamber of Commerce Japan, ACCJ), family social activities, cultural experiences (festivals, travel within Japan). Avoiding isolation is important for sustained success.

6. Mid-assignment review: 12-18 months in, formal review of assignment progress, challenges, adjustments needed. This is a critical point for intervention if problems are developing.

Repatriation (6-12 months before return):

1. Role planning: BEFORE the assignment ends, plan what role the manager will have upon return. Research shows ~25% of repatriated managers leave within one year of return, often because no appropriate role awaits them. Early planning prevents this.

2. Knowledge capture: debrief the expat on lessons learned, relationships built, market insights, and cultural understanding. This knowledge should be transferred to successors and integrated into corporate knowledge.

3. Network maintenance: preserve relationships with Japanese colleagues for ongoing business collaboration. The expat's Japan experience is a long-term asset for the firm.

4. Personal repatriation planning: logistics of return (housing, schools for children returning to US, spouse re-employment), financial transition (tax implications, cost of living change), cultural re-adjustment (reverse culture shock is real).

5. Welcome back process: formal recognition of the assignment, sharing of experiences with home country colleagues, new role assumption with clear expectations.

Japan-specific repatriation considerations: Japan expats often struggle with reverse culture shock because Japanese business culture is so different. The faster pace and less formal communication of American business can be disorienting. A structured re-entry process helps.

Success metrics:

A successful Japan assignment should produce:
- Technical objectives met (subsidiary growth, market entry, strategic goals)
- Relationships built with Japanese stakeholders that outlast the specific assignment
- Knowledge transferred in both directions (Japan expertise to US, US capabilities to Japan)
- Manager career-advanced through the experience
- Successor prepared (ideally a Japanese national) for the role
- Family well-adjusted and committed to future international opportunities

Budget implications:

A 3-year Japan assignment typically costs the company $1.5-2.5 million in total (beyond what the manager would have been paid domestically). This is a significant investment that should be evaluated like any other capital decision. Firms that shortcut the investment (inadequate training, weak support, poor repatriation) often see assignments fail, wasting the investment and producing worse outcomes than not sending anyone at all.

Alternative: Consider whether sending an expat is even the right choice. For some roles, hiring a Japanese national or third-country national with Japan experience might be more effective and less expensive. The firm should consciously choose expat assignment rather than default to it.

rubric.key: Complete lifecycle coverage (selection, preparation, compensation, support, repatriation)
Japan-specific considerations throughout
Realistic cost and risk analysis

questions.question: A US firm is evaluating two countries for a new manufacturing facility: Vietnam (labor $3/hour, $50M investment, moderate political risk, 20% corporate tax rate) and Mexico (labor $5/hour, $60M investment, lower political risk, 30% corporate tax rate). The facility will produce goods worth $100M annually. How should the firm approach this decision?
questions.answer:
International capital budgeting requires a structured analysis that goes beyond simple cost comparison. The decision involves quantitative evaluation of cash flows, qualitative assessment of strategic factors, and risk-adjusted comparison of alternatives.

Step 1: Build cash flow projections for each country.

Assume 10-year analysis period with end-of-life residual values.

Vietnam - baseline projections:
- Initial investment: $50 million at year 0
- Annual revenue: $100 million
- Labor cost: depends on labor intensity. Assume 20% labor-intensive ($20M labor budget at $3/hour vs. assume labor productivity ratio). Operating costs including labor: ~$70M
- Operating profit: $30M pre-tax
- Tax at 20%: $6M
- After-tax profit: $24M
- Depreciation: ~$5M/year (10-year straight line)
- Annual operating cash flow: ~$29M

Mexico - baseline projections:
- Initial investment: $60 million at year 0
- Annual revenue: $100 million
- Labor cost: higher than Vietnam. Operating costs: ~$73M (higher labor, but more efficient logistics)
- Operating profit: $27M pre-tax
- Tax at 30%: $8.1M
- After-tax profit: $18.9M
- Depreciation: ~$6M/year
- Annual operating cash flow: ~$24.9M

Step 2: Calculate NPV for each country.

Required return (cost of capital): depends on risk profile. Assume 12% for Vietnam (higher risk) and 10% for Mexico (lower risk).

Vietnam NPV:
Initial: -$50M
Annual cash flows: $29M for 10 years
PV at 12%: $29M x 5.650 = $163.85M
Net NPV: $163.85M - $50M = $113.85M

Mexico NPV:
Initial: -$60M
Annual cash flows: $24.9M for 10 years
PV at 10%: $24.9M x 6.145 = $153.01M
Net NPV: $153.01M - $60M = $93.01M

On a pure NPV basis, Vietnam looks better ($113.85M vs $93.01M).

Step 3: Adjust for additional considerations.

NPV alone is insufficient. Multiple additional factors affect the decision:

1. Transportation and logistics: Mexico is much closer to the US market, reducing shipping time and cost. Vietnam manufacturing requires long ocean shipping (2-6 weeks). For time-sensitive goods or high-value products, Mexico's proximity is a significant advantage. If we factor in $10M/year higher logistics costs from Vietnam, the NPV difference narrows substantially.

2. Political risk: Vietnam's higher political risk should be reflected in the discount rate (already done with 12% vs 10%), but catastrophic risks (expropriation, export controls, civil unrest, trade policy changes) may not be fully captured by a 2 percentage point adjustment. Historical precedents (US-Vietnam relations, Vietnam's relationship with China, Southeast Asian political dynamics) should be evaluated.

3. Currency risk: Vietnamese dong has been historically more volatile than Mexican peso against the US dollar. Currency depreciation would reduce the dollar value of Vietnamese operations. Hedging is possible but adds cost.

4. Labor force considerations: Vietnam has a young, growing, increasingly skilled workforce. Mexico has more manufacturing experience but rising labor costs. Long-term trends may favor Vietnam for continued growth; near-term Mexico for operational stability.

5. Supply chain ecosystem: Mexico's proximity to US allows integration with US suppliers and customers. Vietnam is integrated into Southeast Asian supply chains (electronics, textiles, shoes). The right choice depends on the specific industry and supply chain requirements.

6. Trade agreement benefits: Mexico is in USMCA (formerly NAFTA), providing zero-tariff access to US and Canadian markets. Vietnam has CPTPP and bilateral agreements but with different terms. USMCA benefits substantially favor Mexico for products destined for the North American market.

7. Talent availability: Engineering and management talent differs. Mexico has a larger established manufacturing base; Vietnam is growing but has fewer experienced managers.

8. Government incentives: Both countries offer investment incentives. Specific terms should be negotiated - tax holidays, land at favorable terms, streamlined permits, utility guarantees. These can significantly affect returns.

9. Sustainability and ESG: Vietnam's electricity is coal-heavy (higher Scope 2 emissions); Mexico has more renewable potential and growing renewable deployment. For firms with net-zero commitments, this matters.

10. Proximity to customers vs suppliers: Does the firm's value chain favor being near inputs or near outputs? For consumer goods sold in North America, Mexico wins; for components shipped to Asian assembly plants, Vietnam wins.

Step 4: Scenario analysis.

Base case: Use the projections above. Vietnam's NPV exceeds Mexico by ~$20M.

Pessimistic scenarios:
- Vietnam labor cost rises to $5/hour by year 5 (catching up to Mexico today): NPV drops significantly.
- US tariffs on Vietnamese imports: could eliminate Vietnam advantage entirely.
- Vietnamese currency crisis: Vietnamese dong depreciates 30%, reducing dollar value of operations.
- Political disruption in Vietnam or regional conflict: catastrophic loss.

Optimistic scenarios:
- Mexico tax rate cut under future government.
- US-Mexico trade tensions (unlikely) or border issues.
- Labor productivity improvements in Vietnam exceeding projections.

Step 5: Strategic considerations.

Beyond NPV, strategic factors should weight the decision:
- Does the firm need to diversify production geography (not over-concentrate in any single location)?
- Does the firm have management capability to succeed in Vietnam (cultural distance, language, local partnerships) vs Mexico (more familiar environment for US firms)?
- Is this the firm's first international manufacturing? If so, starting with the less complex option (Mexico, with its proximity and familiar regulatory environment) may reduce risk.
- Growth plans: Is the firm planning to serve Asian markets from this facility (favoring Vietnam) or North American markets (favoring Mexico)?

Step 6: Recommendation framework.

Quantitative favorable to Vietnam: The NPV calculation, before adjustments, favors Vietnam by ~$20M.

Qualitative factors mixed:
- Mexico: easier entry, lower risk, logistics advantages for US market, stable environment.
- Vietnam: larger labor advantage, stronger growth trajectory, access to Asian markets.

My recommendation: For a US firm entering international manufacturing for the first time, and for products primarily serving the North American market, Mexico is likely the better choice despite lower projected NPV. The reduced risk, faster time to market, and ease of management justify the cost. The lower NPV buffer provides margin against execution errors that would be more consequential in the harder-to-manage Vietnam environment.

For a US firm already experienced internationally, planning to serve both Asian and North American markets, and with appetite for risk, Vietnam may be preferable for its larger quantitative advantage and future growth potential.

For a sophisticated multinational, the answer may be 'both' - build in Mexico for North American serving and Vietnam for Asian serving and risk diversification. Pure cost comparison misses the strategic value of a diversified global production footprint.

Key lesson: International capital budgeting requires structured quantitative analysis AND honest qualitative assessment. Pure NPV comparison misses strategic factors that often dominate the decision. The analytical challenge is integrating both types of factors into a defensible recommendation.

rubric.key: Structured NPV analysis with reasonable assumptions
Multiple strategic and qualitative factors beyond NPV
Scenario analysis and risk consideration

questions.question: Explain how a firm should assess institutional voids in an emerging market entry decision. Use India or Nigeria as an example.
questions.answer:
Khanna and Palepu's institutional voids framework identifies categories of missing or weak market infrastructure typical of emerging markets. Firms entering these markets must diagnose which voids matter for their business and develop strategies to work around or fill them.

Categories of institutional voids:

1. Information problems: weak or unreliable information about customers, suppliers, competitors, and market conditions. Developed markets have credit rating agencies, specialized business press, market research firms, independent analysts, consumer databases, and financial statements of competitors. Emerging markets often lack these or have weaker versions.

2. Missing or weak contract enforcement: courts are slow, corrupt, or unreliable. Contracts cannot be enforced efficiently through legal mechanisms, so parties must rely on relationships, reputation, or alternative enforcement methods.

3. Weak financial markets: capital is scarce, expensive, or rationed. Stock markets are shallow, bond markets may not exist, banking is concentrated or inefficient, and cross-border capital flows may be restricted.

4. Labor market imperfections: workforce skills may be scarce, training systems weak, labor mobility restricted, or employer-employee relationships governed by different norms.

5. Weak distribution infrastructure: getting goods to customers is complicated by poor roads, underdeveloped retail networks, fragmented wholesalers, unreliable logistics, and last-mile challenges.

6. Regulatory uncertainty: rules change frequently, enforcement is inconsistent, and government decisions can be unpredictable. Corruption may be required for basic operations.

India example:

For a US consumer goods firm entering India:

Information voids:
- Consumer data is fragmented. Credit rating data is limited. Market research must be conducted internally or through specialized Indian firms.
- Response: build internal research capability, partner with Indian market research firms (Nielsen India, IMRB), use mobile data as a proxy for consumer behavior where possible.

Contract enforcement:
- Indian courts are notoriously slow. Legal disputes can take 10+ years to resolve. Business disputes are typically handled through relationships and alternative dispute resolution.
- Response: build long-term relationships with suppliers and distributors; use reputation-based enforcement; include arbitration clauses in contracts; avoid litigation where possible; structure transactions to reduce dispute likelihood.

Financial markets:
- Domestic capital is available but expensive. Stock markets are more developed than most emerging markets (Bombay Stock Exchange is large) but bond markets are shallow.
- Response: use parent company financing from US. Consider Indian bank loans for local currency needs. Hedge against Indian rupee volatility.

Labor markets:
- Large but highly variable workforce. English-speaking educated workers are available but compete with global opportunities. Skilled technical workers (IT, engineering) are well-developed in India. Unskilled manufacturing labor is plentiful. Labor laws are complex and vary by state.
- Response: partner with local HR firms, invest in training, use reputation and compensation to attract talent, respect state-specific labor laws.

Distribution infrastructure:
- India has millions of small retailers (kirana stores) and limited modern retail. Supply chains must handle fragmented, small-quantity distribution to thousands of tiny shops.
- Response: use traditional wholesale networks for kirana distribution; partner with modern retail chains (Reliance, More, DMart) for organized channels; use e-commerce platforms (Flipkart, Amazon India) for urban markets. Hindustan Unilever's 'Project Shakti' enrolls rural women as distributors to reach villages that wholesalers cannot economically serve.

Regulatory environment:
- Indian regulations are complex and state-specific. GST (Goods and Services Tax, implemented 2017) has simplified some aspects but complexity remains. Labor laws, land acquisition laws, and environmental regulations vary.
- Response: hire Indian regulatory specialists, engage with industry associations, build relationships with government stakeholders, comply rigorously (FCPA risk is serious for US firms paying bribes).

Political and macro environment:
- India has a stable democracy with alternating governments. Some states are more business-friendly than others. Currency has been moderately stable.
- Response: consider multiple state locations, build political relationships at federal and state levels, diversify risks.

Strategic approach for a US firm:

1. Diagnose voids systematically: identify which voids matter most for your specific business and target customers. A pharmaceutical company cares most about drug approval processes; a consumer goods company cares most about distribution and branding.

2. Fill voids internally where strategic: build internal capabilities for critical functions (market research, compliance, HR development) rather than depending on weak external institutions.

3. Partner with local intermediaries where possible: Indian distributors know local markets; Indian consultants can navigate regulation; Indian recruiters can find talent.

4. Accept longer timelines and higher uncertainty: Indian business moves at its own pace. Plans must accommodate slower decision-making, longer sales cycles, and occasional surprises.

5. Build for the long term: short-term thinking produces failure in India. Firms that invest in relationships, knowledge, and capabilities over years build durable positions.

6. Learn from other multinationals: Coca-Cola, Unilever, P&G, PepsiCo, Ford, Hyundai, and others have decades of Indian experience. Their successes and failures provide valuable lessons.

Nigeria would have similar but more severe voids: weaker courts, weaker financial markets, more unreliable infrastructure, more unpredictable regulation, more ethnic and regional complexity. Entering Nigeria requires more institutional work than entering India, but the opportunity (Africa's largest market) can justify the investment.

Key principle: institutional voids are not obstacles to be wished away - they are the business environment. Successful entry requires strategies that work WITH the voids (partnering, relationship-building, internal capability) rather than demanding Western-style institutions that don't exist. Firms that try to operate in emerging markets as if they were developed markets typically fail.

rubric.key: Clear explanation of institutional voids framework
Specific examples from India or Nigeria with strategic responses
Principle of working with voids rather than against them

quizzes.topic: Geopolitics, Sustainability, and Future of International Business

questions.question: Graham Allison's 'Thucydides Trap' argues that rising powers challenging established powers historically produce war in 12 of 16 cases. How should this analysis affect US firm strategy toward China over the next decade?
questions.answer:
Graham Allison's Destined for War (2017) applied the Thucydides Trap concept - the dangerous dynamic when a rising power threatens an established power - to US-China relations. His analysis identified 16 historical cases of rising-established power competition over 500 years; in 12 of them, the competition ended in war. This framework has become influential in strategic discussions and should affect how US firms plan their China strategies over the next decade.

What Allison's analysis suggests:

1. Structural tensions are real and growing. The US has been the dominant power since 1945; China has risen dramatically since 1990 and is now the world's second-largest economy. This power shift creates tensions regardless of specific policies or personalities, because it challenges existing norms, alliances, and institutions that the established power built.

2. Historical track record is grim. The 4 cases that avoided war did so through deliberate management (UK-Germany before WWI failed; UK-US in the early 20th century succeeded, partly due to cultural similarities and complementary interests). The default outcome has been war.

3. Specific flashpoints exist. Taiwan is the most obvious - China has declared reunification a core national interest; the US has committed under the Taiwan Relations Act to provide defense assistance. The South China Sea, North Korea, the Senkaku Islands, Korean peninsula, and cyber domain all create potential triggers for escalation.

4. Technology competition is accelerating. Semiconductors, AI, quantum computing, biotechnology, and space are viewed as decisive for future military and economic power. Both countries are pursuing technological supremacy with national security logic.

5. Economic interdependence is not enough to prevent conflict. Pre-WWI Britain and Germany had extensive trade and financial relationships but fought a catastrophic war anyway. Interdependence reduces war's probability but does not eliminate it when national interests diverge.

Implications for US firm strategy:

1. Plan for continued decoupling, not reintegration. The assumption of 1990-2015 - that China would continue liberalizing and integrating with the global economy - has been invalidated. US firms should plan for continued divergence: more export controls on China, more investment restrictions, more supply chain scrutiny, more dual-use technology concerns. A firm that bets on reintegration and strategic partnership with China is betting against the observable trend.

2. Reduce strategic dependencies on China. For critical inputs, capabilities, or markets, excessive China dependence creates vulnerability. Diversification should be pursued even at cost. Apple's gradual shift from full Chinese iPhone assembly to India and Vietnam represents this logic.

3. Prepare for worst-case scenarios. Scenario planning should include: US-China military confrontation over Taiwan, comprehensive sanctions on China (similar to those on Russia after 2022), Chinese retaliation against Western firms, complete technology decoupling, capital controls limiting currency repatriation. These scenarios may not happen, but firms should know what they would do if they did.

4. Maintain optionality in investment decisions. When possible, build operations that can be moved, converted, or scaled down rather than making extremely location-specific commitments. Leasing rather than building; using contract manufacturers rather than owned facilities; licensing rather than direct investment.

5. Strengthen home-country and allied country operations. The CHIPS Act and Inflation Reduction Act create incentives to build or expand US manufacturing. Some capabilities should be re-shored even at higher cost, particularly those with national security implications. Capability in allied countries (Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, EU, UK, Canada, Australia) provides alternatives.

6. Be cautious about technology transfer to China. IP should be protected more rigorously. Joint ventures that require technology sharing should be reviewed. Export controls must be complied with even when they reduce commercial opportunities.

7. Monitor political and military signals carefully. Government relations capability matters more now than in a stable international environment. Firms need to understand US administration policy, Chinese government posture, Taiwan situation, and ally positions. Passive compliance is not enough - active engagement with policymakers is necessary for strategic firms.

8. Avoid over-reliance on Chinese market revenue. Firms that depend on Chinese consumers for a major share of profits face real strategic risk. Not every firm should abandon China, but they should know their exposure and plan mitigation.

9. Build relationships in emerging markets beyond China. India, Vietnam, Indonesia, Brazil, Mexico represent the next wave of emerging markets. Firms that diversify their emerging market portfolio beyond China have optionality if China dependence becomes untenable.

10. Recognize that the situation may resolve peacefully. Despite Allison's grim statistics, peace remains the most desirable outcome and is not impossible. The goal is resilience if conflict comes and productive engagement if it does not.

Specific company strategies:

Apple: continue diversifying manufacturing to India and Vietnam (goal: ~30% outside China by 2027) while maintaining Chinese market presence and operations. Maintain relationships with Chinese political and business leadership. Hedge by ensuring key operations can function without Chinese access.

Microsoft: has reduced China research operations (closed major AI lab in 2024), limits cutting-edge technology transfer, maintains Chinese cloud operations under Chinese ownership rules. Accepts reduced China profit potential for reduced risk.

Tesla: maintains significant Chinese manufacturing at Shanghai Gigafactory but has announced plans to accelerate production in Berlin, Mexico, and other locations. Musk's personal relationship with Chinese leadership helps manage political risk.

Semiconductor firms (Nvidia, AMD, Intel): face direct export restrictions on advanced chips to China. Must navigate compliance while preserving legitimate business. Investment in non-China manufacturing (US CHIPS Act) is strategic hedge.

Financial services: US banks in China face increasing friction. JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs reduced Chinese operations or face political pressure at home. Chinese banks similarly face restrictions in the US.

Key skepticism about Allison's framework:

Allison's specific case count (12 of 16 ending in war) has been criticized by historians who question the case selection and categorization. The framework is suggestive rather than predictive. Specific decisions by leaders, random events, and unforeseen factors may matter more than deterministic structural forces.

However, even a skeptical view of Thucydides Trap accepts that US-China tensions are real, growing, and consequential. Whether or not war results, the relationship will not return to pre-2016 patterns. Firms should plan accordingly.

Conclusion: US firms cannot treat Chinese operations, supply chains, and markets as routine business decisions. Strategic thinking, government relations, risk management, and diversification are now essential. The cost of preparedness is significant but the cost of unpreparedness could be catastrophic.

rubric.key: Clear explanation of Thucydides Trap framework with historical basis
Specific strategic implications for US firms with examples
Balanced consideration including counterarguments and uncertainties

questions.question: The EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) imposes carbon taxes on certain imports from countries with weaker climate policies. How does CBAM affect international business strategy?
questions.answer:
The EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) is one of the most significant climate-related trade measures implemented to date. It represents the first major example of 'climate-adjusted trade' - making trade policy conditional on climate policy alignment. CBAM has major implications for international business strategy.

What CBAM is:

CBAM imposes a carbon price on imports of certain carbon-intensive goods entering the EU from countries without comparable climate policies. Initially covered goods (as of 2023-2026 phase-in) include iron and steel, aluminum, cement, fertilizers, electricity, and hydrogen. The mechanism requires importers to report the embedded carbon emissions of imports and purchase CBAM certificates (at the EU Emissions Trading System price, currently around €80-100 per ton CO2) corresponding to those emissions.

The basic logic: the EU has strong climate policies (ETS, emissions reduction targets, renewable energy mandates) that create costs for EU producers. Without CBAM, carbon-intensive production could simply move from the EU to countries with weaker climate policies (carbon leakage), undermining climate goals while hurting EU industry. CBAM levels the playing field by making imports bear similar carbon costs.

Implementation timeline:
- 2023-2025: transitional phase with reporting requirements but no financial obligation
- 2026: full implementation with certificate purchases required
- 2030s: likely expansion to additional product categories

Implications for international business:

1. Direct cost impact on carbon-intensive exporters to the EU.

Firms exporting covered products to the EU face new costs equivalent to the difference between their production carbon intensity and the EU-established benchmark, multiplied by the CBAM certificate price. For steel producers in countries with coal-heavy electricity (China, India), the cost could be $50-150 per ton of steel, significantly affecting price competitiveness.

Chinese steel exports to the EU face roughly $80-120/ton CBAM cost based on Chinese steel's emission intensity (approximately 2.0 tons CO2 per ton of steel vs the EU benchmark of around 1.3 tons). This is a significant competitive disadvantage.

Implication: exporters must either reduce their emissions (hard and slow), absorb the cost (reducing profit), or lose market share to cleaner competitors.

2. Investment in decarbonization to maintain competitiveness.

Producers serving the EU market have strong incentive to invest in lower-carbon production methods: switching to renewable electricity, using green hydrogen instead of coal in steelmaking (still early-stage), carbon capture, or alternative materials.

Examples: ArcelorMittal has announced major investments in hydrogen-based steelmaking. SSAB in Sweden has committed to carbon-free steel by 2026. Some producers are locating new capacity near renewable energy sources to reduce emissions.

Firms that invest early in decarbonization will have competitive advantage in EU and other carbon-regulated markets; firms that delay will face rising costs.

3. Supply chain restructuring.

Multinational firms must track carbon emissions throughout their supply chains to comply with CBAM reporting requirements. This requires:
- Scope 3 emissions measurement (emissions from suppliers and supply chain)
- Supplier-specific emissions data collection
- Audit and verification systems
- Integration with ERP and procurement systems

Some firms will respond by choosing suppliers with lower carbon emissions, even if prices are higher. This creates business opportunities for clean producers (green steel, renewable energy manufacturers) and challenges for traditional high-carbon suppliers.

4. Geopolitical and trade tensions.

CBAM has been criticized by many non-EU countries as a disguised protectionist measure and a violation of WTO principles. China, India, and other developing countries have argued that CBAM imposes Western climate policies on countries with different development priorities and responsibilities. Discussions at COP and WTO continue about whether and how to challenge CBAM.

Implication: international business operates in an environment where climate policy has become trade policy. Firms must monitor these tensions and plan for potential retaliation (e.g., Chinese carbon border tariffs on EU exports), escalation (WTO disputes), or harmonization (joint climate-trade frameworks).

5. Precedent for other countries.

The EU's CBAM is the first but likely not the last such mechanism. The US, UK, Canada, Australia, and Japan are considering similar measures. If multiple major economies implement CBAMs, carbon pricing becomes effectively global for traded goods, accelerating the transition to low-carbon production.

Implication: strategies that work under EU CBAM should also work under emerging US or UK CBAMs. Firms preparing for the EU are de facto preparing for a global trend.

6. Competitive advantage for clean producers.

Firms already producing with low-carbon methods (hydropower-based aluminum, renewable-powered cement, scrap-based steel, green hydrogen) gain competitive advantage in CBAM-regulated markets. These firms can capture premium pricing while carbon-intensive competitors struggle.

Examples: Alcoa's Canadian operations using hydropower; Brazil's steel industry with higher charcoal use; Norway's aluminum and fertilizer industries with renewable electricity.

7. Need for accurate emissions reporting.

CBAM requires detailed, verified emissions reporting. Firms that cannot provide accurate data default to worst-case assumptions (highest emission estimates for their production process), which maximizes costs. Investment in emissions measurement infrastructure is essential.

8. Product redesign and alternative sourcing.

Some firms may redesign products to use fewer carbon-intensive inputs. Cement-based construction could shift to wood (cross-laminated timber). Steel-heavy products could use more aluminum or composites (though CBAM covers aluminum too). Chemical-intensive agriculture could shift toward regenerative practices.

9. Timing considerations.

Early movers in decarbonization can capture advantage before the regulatory pressure peaks. Late movers will face rising costs, stranded assets, and competitive disadvantage.

Weaknesses and uncertainties:

- WTO compatibility is disputed. CBAM could be challenged and potentially modified or struck down.
- Verification of foreign emissions is technically challenging. Data quality may be lower than domestic.
- Transition costs in developing countries are significant. Humanitarian and development arguments may lead to exemptions or financial support.
- The carbon price in the ETS may rise significantly, increasing CBAM costs over time.
- Enforcement and implementation face practical challenges.

Synthesis:

CBAM represents a structural shift in international trade: climate policy is now trade policy. Firms with international operations must integrate carbon considerations into their strategy - from investment decisions to supplier selection to product design to market positioning. Firms that treat climate as a compliance afterthought will face growing costs and declining competitiveness. Firms that make climate a central strategic consideration can capture advantage as the transition accelerates.

This is not just about the EU. CBAM is a signal of where international trade is headed. Any strategy focused solely on short-term cost optimization will miss the longer-term competitive dynamics that climate regulation is creating.

rubric.key: Clear explanation of CBAM mechanism and its scope
Multiple strategic implications with specific examples
Discussion of limitations, uncertainties, and broader trends

questions.question: Identify three ways that artificial intelligence is reshaping international business strategy in 2026, with specific examples.
questions.answer:
The emergence of generative AI (ChatGPT released November 2022, followed by rapid advancement in 2023-2026) is reshaping international business strategy across multiple dimensions. Three significant ways AI is affecting global strategy:

1. Language and cultural barriers are being lowered.

AI-powered translation has improved dramatically. Modern large language models can translate between most major languages with accuracy approaching professional human translators for routine business communications. Real-time voice translation through tools like Microsoft Teams Intelligent Interpretation, Google Meet's real-time translation, and specialized tools from Meta and others makes cross-language meetings possible without human interpreters.

Impact: International business becomes more accessible to smaller firms without dedicated translation resources. Cross-border teams can collaborate more easily. Customer service can be delivered in dozens of languages without hiring native speakers for each. Legal and technical documents can be translated quickly (though still with human verification for high-stakes content).

Specific example: A mid-sized US manufacturer can now provide Japanese-language customer support using AI without hiring Japanese speakers, reducing the barrier to entering the Japanese market. Before 2022, this would have required expensive translation services or a local partner; now it costs pennies per interaction.

Limits: AI translation is not perfect. Cultural nuances, legal terminology, and complex technical concepts still require human expertise. High-stakes negotiations, legal contracts, and sensitive customer interactions should not rely solely on AI. But for routine communications, AI has dramatically reduced the language barrier that historically made international expansion expensive and slow.

2. AI is becoming a core battleground in US-China competition.

The US government views AI leadership as critical to national security and economic competitiveness. The October 2022 export controls on advanced semiconductor manufacturing equipment and AI chips explicitly targeted Chinese AI capability. Subsequent expansions in 2023 and 2024 have extended restrictions. The Chinese government views AI leadership as essential and is investing massively in domestic capabilities through Huawei, Baidu, Alibaba, and hundreds of startups.

Impact: International business in AI-related industries faces stark choices. Firms must navigate export controls, investment screening, and national security considerations. Firms serving both US and Chinese markets must understand which technologies can flow which direction and what the penalties for violations are. Decoupling between US and Chinese AI ecosystems is deepening.

Specific example: Nvidia, the leading AI chip company, has had to modify its products for the Chinese market to comply with US export controls. First it offered the H800 chip (slightly below the threshold for restricted performance). When US controls tightened in October 2023, H800 was restricted. Nvidia then offered the H20 chip with further reduced performance. This cat-and-mouse with regulation has cost Nvidia billions in Chinese revenue and forced China to accelerate domestic chip development (Huawei's Ascend chips).

Broader impact: Firms in AI-adjacent industries (cloud computing, software, data analytics, biotech, advanced manufacturing) must consider whether they have dual-use technology that could be subject to export controls. Compliance systems, customer screening, and technology classification become operational necessities.

3. AI is enabling new business models and competitive advantages in global operations.

Multinational firms are using AI for:

- Global demand forecasting: AI analyzes patterns across markets to predict demand more accurately, enabling better inventory management and production planning. Walmart, Amazon, and major retailers use AI for global supply chain optimization.

- Fraud detection and compliance: AI monitors transactions across multiple countries to detect suspicious patterns, enabling better compliance with anti-money laundering, FCPA, sanctions, and tax regulations. Financial institutions and multinationals use this extensively.

- Market research and competitive intelligence: AI can analyze foreign-language news, social media, and public records to identify competitive threats, customer sentiment, and market opportunities across countries that would be prohibitively expensive to monitor manually.

- Personalization at scale: Netflix uses AI to recommend content across 190+ countries with local preferences. Amazon personalizes product recommendations for customers globally. This enables global scale with local relevance - the transnational strategy executed through technology.

- Customer service in emerging markets: AI chatbots can provide basic customer service in local languages at costs that enable serving small markets that were previously unprofitable to serve.

- Content generation for localization: Marketing content, product descriptions, and website material can be generated and localized for dozens of markets at a fraction of previous cost.

Specific example: Duolingo has expanded to teach over 40 languages with AI-generated content, personalized practice, and AI tutoring (Duolingo Max using GPT-4). This is a business model that would have been impossible without AI - creating high-quality language learning content for 40+ languages through traditional human curriculum development would have taken decades.

Broader strategic implications:

- Firms must decide their AI strategy: build internal AI capability, partner with AI providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Microsoft, Chinese alternatives), or buy AI services from specialists.
- Competitive dynamics are shifting faster. AI can commoditize skills and functions that were previously sources of advantage. Firms must continually adapt or lose competitive position.
- Jobs and skills are being reshaped. Middle-skill knowledge work (administrative, analytical, communications) is being automated. This has implications for labor markets, talent strategies, and the composition of multinational workforces.
- Data becomes a strategic asset. Firms with proprietary data (customer interactions, transactions, operations) can train AI models that provide competitive advantage. Data strategy is strategy.
- Regulation is emerging. EU AI Act (2024), US executive orders, and Chinese AI regulations create a patchwork of compliance requirements. International firms must navigate different rules in different jurisdictions.
- AI safety and ethics concerns affect deployment. Bias, privacy, transparency, and accountability issues shape how AI can be used in different markets.

Not yet clear but potentially important:

- Will AI favor large incumbent firms (who have data, capital, and scale) or enable smaller firms to compete (by reducing costs and complexity)?
- Will AI lead to more or less globalization? Lower language/coordination costs favor more globalization; concerns about strategic autonomy favor more fragmentation.
- How will AI affect emerging market development? AI could skip steps in development (mobile money, telemedicine, AI-assisted education) or could widen gaps between AI-capable and AI-lagging countries.

Strategic priority: Every international business strategy should explicitly consider AI implications - both opportunities and risks. Firms that treat AI as an IT issue rather than a strategic issue will be outcompeted by firms that integrate AI into core strategy. The pace of change means strategies developed in 2023 may already be outdated; continuous adaptation is essential.

rubric.key: Three substantive ways AI reshapes international business
Specific current examples (2023-2026)
Strategic implications and priorities for firms

major_assignments.title: Country and Market Analysis for International Entry
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive analysis of a country as a potential market for a specific firm's entry. Apply PESTEL, national difference frameworks (political/economic/legal/cultural), and Porter's diamond. Produce a consulting-grade report recommending whether and how to enter the target market.
objectives: Apply PESTEL and national difference frameworks to a real country
Diagnose institutional voids and cultural considerations
Evaluate market attractiveness using integrated analysis
deliverables: Executive summary (1 page) with key findings and recommendation
Country analysis (3-4 pages): PESTEL, political/economic/legal system, cultural dimensions using Hofstede and Hall frameworks
Market analysis (2-3 pages): industry-specific factors, competition, distribution, customers
Institutional voids analysis (2 pages): gaps identified and strategic responses
Entry mode recommendation (2-3 pages): analysis of options and justified recommendation
Risk assessment (1-2 pages): political, economic, operational risks
Appendix with data sources and references

criteria: Rigorous PESTEL with strategic relevance filtering
Entry mode recommendation justified using OLI or similar framework
Country and market analysis using multiple frameworks

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected at all lectures and mandatory for case discussions and guest speaker sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may reduce the participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without documented emergency.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts and case approaches is encouraged. All individual assignments must be your own work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Copying case analyses or international business plans from consulting reports or other students constitutes academic misconduct.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Excel for financial analysis, access to international trade databases (UNCTAD, WTO, World Bank), and the course textbook. Lab computers are available.
